entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 3 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

From reactive triage to proactive monitoring — a multi-agent system with a router that delegates SM21 system log errors and SM37 job failures to specialized sub-agents, correlates findings across log types, and escalates autonomously.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 3 introduces the multi-agent pattern — a router agent that classifies incoming SAP errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents: one for SM21 system log analysis, one for SM37 job failure triage. The router also identifies correlations across log types that neither sub-agent sees alone. The article covers two stages: Stage A reactive triage (triggered on demand) and Stage B proactive monitoring (continuous, schedule-driven). This is the architectural foundation for the multi-agent orchestration in Part 6.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Parts 1 and 2 built single agents — a data quality assessor and a migration assessment agent with conditional routing. Part 3 introduces multi-agent coordination: a router agent that classifies errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents. This pattern is the core building block for Part 6, where all agents in this series work together under a unified orchestrator.

The exception log triage use case is ideal for introducing multi-agent patterns because SAP error landscapes involve genuinely different log types that require different analysis approaches. A single agent handling SM21 and SM37 in the same reasoning chain produces lower quality findings than two specialized agents, each focused on their domain, coordinated by a router that sees the full picture.

WHY MULTI-AGENT FOR SAP LOG TRIAGE 

Why Log Triage Benefits from Multi-Agent Architecture

SAP exception triage in production environments involves multiple log sources that are related but structurally different:

Log Type

What It Contains

Who Cares

SM21 System Log

Platform-level errors — work process failures, memory issues, RFC connection problems, kernel errors. Context is technical infrastructure.

SAP Basis / Platform team

SM37 Job Log

Business process failures — scheduled job errors, batch processing failures, data load problems. Context is business process continuity.

Functional / Operations team

Correlation layer

When an SM37 job fails because of an SM21 RFC connection error — neither log alone tells the full story. The router sees both.

Both teams need joint view

A single agent reasoning over both log types simultaneously tends to conflate the contexts — applying infrastructure reasoning to business process errors and vice versa. Specialized sub-agents with a coordinating router produce more accurate findings and clearer escalation paths.

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Router + Sub-Agent Architecture

Error batch input → Router agent classifies → SM21 sub-agent → Correlate findings → Severity score + escalation
		→	SM37 sub-agent	→

The router agent has three responsibilities:

  • Classify incoming errors — determine which log type each error belongs to
  • Delegate to the appropriate specialized sub-agent
  • Correlate findings across both sub-agents — identify cases where SM21 infrastructure errors are causing SM37 job failures

 

Agent

Responsibility

Router agent

Classifies errors, delegates to sub-agents, correlates cross-log findings, produces unified severity score

SM21 sub-agent

Analyzes system log errors — identifies platform issues, RFC failures, memory problems, kernel errors

SM37 sub-agent

Analyzes job log failures — identifies which jobs failed, why, business process impact, retry feasibility

STATE SCHEMA AND TOOLS 

State Schema and Tool Definitions

Triage State Schema
# triage_agent.py — multi-agent exception log triage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class TriageState(TypedDict):
    raw_errors:         List[dict]   # Incoming error batch
    sm21_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM21 errors
    sm37_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM37 errors
    sm21_findings:      List[dict]   # SM21 sub-agent findings
    sm37_findings:      List[dict]   # SM37 sub-agent findings
    correlations:       List[dict]   # Cross-log correlations
    overall_severity:   str          # CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
    escalation_needed:  bool
    escalation_targets: List[str]    # Which teams to notify
    triage_report:      Optional[str]

Tool Definitions

# tools/triage_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def get_sm21_errors(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM21 system log errors from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            MANDT, DATUM, UZEIT, MTYPE,
            MSGID, MSGNO, MSGTX,
            REPID, ABAP_RUNTIME_ERROR
        FROM SYSLOG
        WHERE DATUM >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        AND MTYPE IN ('E', 'A', 'X')
        -- E=Error, A=Abend, X=Exit
        ORDER BY DATUM DESC, UZEIT DESC
        LIMIT 200
    ''')
    errors = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        errors.append({
            'date': str(row[1]), 'time': str(row[2]),
            'type': row[3], 'message_id': row[4],
            'message_text': row[6], 'program': row[7],
            'runtime_error': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm21_errors': errors, 'count': len(errors)})
 
@tool
def get_sm37_failures(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM37 job failures from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            JOBNAME, JOBCOUNT, STATUS,
            SDLSTRTDT, SDLSTRTTM,
            LASTCHGDT, LASTCHGTM,
            JOBCLASS, PRGNAME
        FROM TBTCO
        WHERE STATUS = 'A'
        -- A = Aborted
        AND SDLSTRTDT >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        ORDER BY LASTCHGDT DESC, LASTCHGTM DESC
        LIMIT 100
    ''')
    failures = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        failures.append({
            'job_name': row[0], 'job_count': row[1],
            'status': row[2], 'start_date': str(row[3]),
            'start_time': str(row[4]), 'job_class': row[7],
            'program': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm37_failures': failures, 'count': len(failures)})
 
@tool
def get_job_log_details(job_name: str, job_count: str) -> str:
    """Get detailed log messages for a specific failed SM37 job."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT MSGTY, MSGID, MSGNO, MSGV1, MSGV2
        FROM TBTCP
        WHERE JOBNAME = '{job_name}'
        AND JOBCOUNT = '{job_count}'
        AND MSGTY IN ('E', 'A')
        ORDER BY LNSNR
    ''')
    messages = [{'type': r[0], 'msg_id': r[1],
                 'msg_no': r[2], 'text': f'{r[3]} {r[4]}'.strip()}
                for r in cursor.fetchall()]
    return json.dumps({'job': job_name, 'messages': messages})
STAGE A: REACTIVE MULTI-AGENT TRIAGE 

Stage A — Reactive Triage on Demand

Stage A is triggered manually or by an integration call — it receives a batch of errors, classifies them, runs sub-agents, and produces a triage report. This is the right pattern for on-call support workflows and integration with ITSM tools like ServiceNow.

Router Agent

# Router: classifies errors and delegates to sub-agents
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
router_prompt = '''You are a SAP exception log router agent.
Your job is to:
1. Fetch recent SM21 system log errors using get_sm21_errors
2. Fetch recent SM37 job failures using get_sm37_failures
3. Count and categorize errors by type and severity
4. Identify the top 3 most critical issues requiring immediate attention
5. Note any patterns suggesting SM21 errors are causing SM37 failures
6. Produce a structured classification summary for sub-agent processing

Be systematic. Fetch both log types before drawing conclusions.
Always explain your classification reasoning.'''
 
router_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors, get_sm37_failures],
    state_modifier=router_prompt
)

SM21 Sub-Agent

# SM21 specialist: deep analysis of system log errors
sm21_prompt = '''You are an SAP Basis expert specializing in SM21 system logs.
Analyze the SM21 errors provided and for each significant error:

1. Identify the root cause (infrastructure, memory, RFC, kernel)
2. Assess business impact — which processes are affected
3. Classify severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
4. Recommend immediate action
5. Recommend preventive measure

Focus on patterns — a single error may be noise,
ten of the same error in one hour is a systemic issue.
Always reference specific error message IDs in your findings.'''
 
sm21_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors],
    state_modifier=sm21_prompt
)

SM37 Sub-Agent

# SM37 specialist: deep analysis of job failures
sm37_prompt = '''You are an SAP operations expert specializing in SM37 job failures.
For each failed job:

1. Use get_job_log_details to retrieve the specific failure messages
2. Identify the root cause (data issue, RFC failure, timeout, authorization)
3. Assess business process impact — what does this job do?
4. Classify severity based on job class and business criticality
5. Determine if the job can be safely restarted or requires fix-first
6. Identify dependent jobs that may be blocked

Job class A = highest priority. Always investigate class A failures first.'''
 
sm37_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm37_failures, get_job_log_details],
    state_modifier=sm37_prompt
)
Stage A Orchestration — Router + Sub-Agents
# Stage A: Orchestrate router + sub-agents reactively
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import json
 
def run_reactive_triage(hours_back: int = 24) -> dict:
    """Run full reactive triage across SM21 and SM37 logs."""
 
    # Step 1: Router classifies and summarizes all errors
    router_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Fetch and classify all SAP errors from the last {hours_back} hours.
            Summarize SM21 system errors and SM37 job failures separately.
            Flag any cases where system errors appear to be causing job failures.
        ''')]
    })
    router_summary = router_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 2: SM21 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm21_result = sm21_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM21 system log errors from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm21_findings = sm21_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 3: SM37 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm37_result = sm37_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM37 job failures from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Get job log details for
            class A failures first. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm37_findings = sm37_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 4: Router correlates findings from both sub-agents
    correlation_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Correlate these findings from both log types and produce
            a unified triage report with:
            1. Overall severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
            2. Top 3 issues requiring immediate action
            3. Cross-log correlations (SM21 errors causing SM37 failures)
            4. Escalation targets (Basis team / Operations team / Both)
            5. Recommended actions in priority order

            SM21 findings: {sm21_findings}
            SM37 findings: {sm37_findings}
        ''')]
    })
 
    return {
        'router_summary': router_summary,
        'sm21_findings': sm21_findings,
        'sm37_findings': sm37_findings,
        'triage_report': correlation_result['messages'][-1].content
    }
 
# Run reactive triage
result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
print(result['triage_report'])

Passing the router summary as context to each sub-agent is the key pattern here. The sub-agents don't operate in isolation — they receive the router's classification as context, which focuses their analysis on the most significant errors rather than treating every log entry with equal weight.

STAGE B: PROACTIVE MONITORING AGENT 

Stage B — Proactive Monitoring with Scheduled Execution

Stage B wraps the Stage A reactive triage in a monitoring loop. The agent runs on a schedule, compares current error patterns against a baseline, and triggers triage automatically when anomalies are detected — without waiting for a human to initiate the process.

Scheduler triggers → Fetch current errors → Compare to baseline	→ Anomaly detected? → Run reactive triage	→ Escalate if needed

Baseline and Anomaly Detection

# monitoring.py — proactive monitoring wrapper
import json, time, os
from datetime import datetime
 
def get_error_baseline() -> dict:
    """Load stored baseline error counts for comparison."""
    # In production: store baseline in HANA or BTP configuration
    return {
        'sm21_hourly_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM21_BASELINE', '10')),
        'sm37_daily_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM37_BASELINE', '5')),
        'anomaly_threshold': 2.0
        # Trigger if errors > 2x baseline
    }
 
def check_for_anomalies(current_counts: dict, baseline: dict) -> dict:
    """Compare current error counts against baseline."""
    sm21_ratio = current_counts.get('sm21_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm21_hourly_avg'], 1)
    sm37_ratio = current_counts.get('sm37_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm37_daily_avg'], 1)
    threshold = baseline['anomaly_threshold']
 
    return {
        'anomaly_detected': sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold,
        'sm21_ratio': round(sm21_ratio, 2),
        'sm37_ratio': round(sm37_ratio, 2),
        'trigger_reason': (
            f'SM21 errors at {sm21_ratio:.1f}x baseline' if sm21_ratio > threshold
            else f'SM37 failures at {sm37_ratio:.1f}x baseline'
        ) if sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold else None
    }
 
def send_escalation(triage_report: str, targets: list):
    """Send escalation via Microsoft Graph API or Teams webhook."""
    # Integration with MS Graph covered in the training automation article
    # For BTP CF deployment: use requests to call Graph API
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Escalation sent to: {targets}')
    print(triage_report[:500])
    # Truncated for logging
 
def run_monitoring_cycle():
    """Single monitoring cycle — run on schedule via CF Scheduler."""
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Starting monitoring cycle...')
 
    # Quick error count check — lightweight, runs every cycle
    sm21_data = json.loads(get_sm21_errors.invoke({'hours_back': 1}))
    sm37_data = json.loads(get_sm37_failures.invoke({'hours_back': 24}))
 
    current_counts = {
        'sm21_count': sm21_data.get('count', 0),
        'sm37_count': sm37_data.get('count', 0)
    }
 
    baseline = get_error_baseline()
    anomaly = check_for_anomalies(current_counts, baseline)
 
    if anomaly['anomaly_detected']:
        print(f'Anomaly detected: {anomaly["trigger_reason"]}')
        # Run full reactive triage
        result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=4)
        # Triage last 4 hours on anomaly
 
        # Escalate if triage confirms high severity
        if 'CRITICAL' in result['triage_report'] or 'HIGH' in result['triage_report']:
            send_escalation(
                result['triage_report'],
                targets=['basis-team@company.com', 'operations@company.com']
            )
    else:
        print(f'No anomaly. SM21: {current_counts["sm21_count"]} errors,
              SM37: {current_counts["sm37_count"]} failures — within baseline.')
 
# BTP CF Flask entry point — triggered by CF Scheduler
from flask import Flask, jsonify
app = Flask(__name__)
 
@app.route('/monitor', methods=['POST'])
def monitor():
    run_monitoring_cycle()
    return jsonify({'status': 'ok', 'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat()})
 
@app.route('/triage', methods=['POST'])
def triage():
    # Manual reactive triage endpoint
    result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
    return jsonify(result)

The baseline values (SM21_BASELINE, SM37_BASELINE) are set as BTP environment variables in your manifest.yml. Start with conservative baselines — set them to your current average error counts — and tighten them over time as you observe the agent's sensitivity. Anomaly thresholds that are too low produce alert fatigue. Start at 2x baseline and adjust based on false positive rate.

STAGE A VS STAGE B 

When to Use Each Pattern

Consideration

Stage A — Reactive

Stage B — Proactive

Trigger

Manual or integration call

Scheduled — runs every N minutes

Use case

On-call support, ITSM integration, ad-hoc investigation

Production monitoring, overnight batch, continuous operations

Cost

Runs only when called — lower LLM cost

Runs on schedule — baseline check is lightweight, full triage only on anomaly

Deployment

REST endpoint on BTP CF

CF Scheduler + REST endpoint on BTP CF

Recommended for

Support teams, incident response workflows

Operations teams, 24x7 production environments

NEW PATTERN: ROUTER + SUB-AGENT COORDINATION 

What This Part Introduces for Part 6

Part 3 introduces the router + sub-agent coordination pattern that Part 6 uses to orchestrate all four series agents together. The key principles that carry forward:

  • The router has a global view — it sees all error types and identifies cross-domain correlations that sub-agents cannot see working independently
  • Sub-agents are specialists — they receive focused context from the router and produce higher quality findings than a generalist agent would
  • Context passing is explicit — each sub-agent receives the router's summary as input context, not just raw data
  • Correlation is the router's unique value — the pattern where SM21 infrastructure errors cause SM37 job failures is only visible to the agent with the cross-log view

In Part 6, this same router pattern becomes the orchestrator that delegates to the migration assessment agent from Part 2, the master data quality agent from Part 4, and the S/4HANA readiness agent from Part 5. The triage agent from this part also participates as a sub-agent when the orchestrator detects operational anomalies during assessment runs.

What's Next — Part 4

  • Part 4: SAP Master Data Quality Agent — detects and prioritizes data quality issues across master data domains (material, vendor, customer), generates a scored remediation plan, and integrates with the HANA vector store RAG pipeline from Series 1 for context-enriched findings.
  • Part 5: S/4HANA Readiness Agent — extends the Part 2 migration assessment with clean core analysis, deprecated function module and BAPI detection, and BAdI/enhancement spot mapping for upgrade impact scoring.
  • Part 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — all four agents coordinated by a unified orchestrator that delegates, synthesizes, and produces a complete SAP landscape intelligence report.

The router + sub-agent pattern from this article is reusable across any SAP domain with multiple related data sources. The same architecture applies to financial audit log analysis, security violation detection across multiple authorization logs, and performance monitoring across work process and database logs.

entry.title: How I Connected Claude AI to My SAP ABAP System Using MCP — A Complete Windows Guide
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP developer community has been buzzing about connecting Claude AI directly to live SAP ABAP systems using MCP (Model Context Protocol) — reading code, modifying objects, creating transports, and activating changes, all from a natural language chat interface.

I tried it myself on Windows, and it works. This blog walks you through exactly how to set it up.

What is MCP?

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is an open standard that lets AI models like Claude connect to external tools and data sources. Think of it as a plugin system for AI — instead of just answering questions from training data, Claude can actually call tools that interact with real systems.

For SAP developers, this means Claude can:

  • Read your actual ABAP code directly from your system
  • Write and modify objects
  • Run syntax checks
  • Create transport requests
  • Activate objects

All without you copy-pasting anything.

The Architecture

 
 
Claude Desktop / Claude Code CLI         ↕ MCP Protocol  ┌──────────────────────────┐  │   mcp-abap-adt           │  ← Read-only (13 tools)  │   mcp-abap-abap-adt-api  │  ← Full CRUD (30 tools)  └──────────────────────────┘         ↕ ADT REST API    Your SAP ABAP System

Both MCP servers were built by Mario Andreschak and communicate with SAP via the same ADT (ABAP Development Tools) API that Eclipse uses under the hood.

Prerequisites

Before starting, you need:

  • Windows 10 or 11 (64-bit)
  • An SAP ABAP system with /sap/bc/adt active in SICF
  • A user with SAP_ADT_DEVELOPER role (or equivalent)
  • A Claude Pro/Max/Team subscription at claude.ai
  • Internet connection

Step 1 — Install Node.js

Node.js is the runtime required by Claude Code CLI and the MCP servers.

  1. Go to https://nodejs.org and download the LTS version
  2. Install with all defaults
  3. Verify in Command Prompt:
 
 
node -v    → v24.x.xnpm -v     → 11.x.x

Step 2 — Install Git for Windows

Git provides the bash shell Claude Code requires on Windows.

  1. Go to https://git-scm.com/downloads/win and download the installer
  2. During setup, on the PATH screen select: "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software"
  3. Complete with all other defaults

Step 3 — Install Claude Code CLI

 
 
cmd
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-codeclaude --version

Then log in:

 
 
cmd
claude

Select option 1 (Claude subscription), log in via browser, return to terminal.

Step 4 — Install mcp-abap-adt (Read-Only MCP Server)

This server gives Claude read access to your SAP system with 13 tools including GetProgram, GetClass, GetFunction, GetTable, SearchObject, and more.

 
 
cmd
mkdir C:\Toolscd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-adtcd mcp-abap-adtnpm installnpm run build

Create a .env file in the folder:

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USERNAME=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register with Claude Code:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-adt/dist/index.jsclaude mcp list

You should see: mcp-abap-adt: ✓ Connected

Step 5 — Install mcp-abap-abap-adt-api (Full CRUD Server)

This is the powerful one — 30 tools including read, write, syntax check, activate, lock/unlock, and transport management.

 
 
cmd
cd C:\Toolsgit clone https://github.com/mario-andreschak/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api.gitcd mcp-abap-abap-adt-apinpm installnpm run build

Create .env (note: slightly different variable names):

 
 
SAP_URL=https://your-sap-system.com:44300SAP_USER=YOUR_USERNAMESAP_PASSWORD=YOUR_PASSWORDSAP_CLIENT=300SAP_LANGUAGE=ENNODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0

Register:

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add mcp-abap-adt-api node C:/Tools/mcp-abap-abap-adt-api/dist/index.js

Step 6 — Setup Claude Desktop (GUI Interface)

Claude Desktop is where it really comes alive — a beautiful chat UI like the LinkedIn video.

  1. Download from https://claude.ai/download
  2. Install and log in
  3. Go to ☰ → Settings → Developer → Edit Config
  4. Replace the content with:
 
 
json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "mcp-abap-adt": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-adt\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USERNAME": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    },
    "mcp-abap-adt-api": {
      "command": "C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe",
      "args": ["C:\\Tools\\mcp-abap-abap-adt-api\\dist\\index.js"],
      "env": {
        "SAP_URL": "https://your-sap-system.com:44300",
        "SAP_USER": "YOUR_USERNAME",
        "SAP_PASSWORD": "YOUR_PASSWORD",
        "SAP_CLIENT": "300",
        "SAP_LANGUAGE": "EN",
        "NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED": "0"
      }
    }
  }
}

⚠️ Windows-specific tip: You MUST use the full path C:\\Program Files\\nodejs\\node.exe — using just "node" does not work in Claude Desktop on Windows. Also note double backslashes \\ in all paths.

  1. Kill Claude in Task Manager and reopen
  2. Go back to Settings → Developer — you should see both servers listed as running ✅

Step 7 — Add Free Official SAP MCP Servers

Bonus: Claude Desktop has a built-in directory of official SAP MCP servers. Click "Connect your tools to Claude" at the bottom of the chat, search for "SAP", and add:

  • SAPUI5 MCP Server — for UI5 development
  • SAP CAP MCP Server — for CAP projects
  • SAP Fiori MCP Server — for Fiori Elements apps

Also add these free hosted servers to Claude Code CLI (no installation needed):

 
 
cmd
claude mcp add --transport http abap-docs https://mcp-abap.marianzeis.de/mcpclaude mcp add --transport http sap-docs https://mcp-sap-docs.marianzeis.de/mcp

These give Claude access to the full ABAP keyword documentation across 8 SAP releases, ABAP cheat sheets, CAP docs, UI5 docs, and SAP Community posts.

Testing It

Open Claude Desktop, create a New Chat, and type:

 
 
Get the source code of program SAPMV45A

Watch Claude automatically call your SAP system, retrieve the source code, and explain it — all in seconds.

For write operations (test on $TMP objects only!):

 
 
Find a simple Z program in $TMP, add a comment at the top saying "Modified by Claude", save and activate it

For complex workflows like the LinkedIn video:

 
 
Connect to my SAP system. Copy class ZCL_SOURCE from package ZPKG_A into ZPKG_B, rename it to ZCL_TARGET, change the carrier_id in the SELECT for /DMO/CONNECTION, create a transport and activate.

Security Notes

  • Never commit .env files to Git
  • Change your SAP password if shared in screenshots
  • Use dedicated service accounts with minimum required authorizations
  • Only test write operations in $TMP before moving to real packages
  • The TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 flag disables SSL validation — use only in dev environments
  • Check your company's AI policy before sending code to external APIs

My Experience

What surprised me most is how Claude handles the entire workflow autonomously. It doesn't just write code — it searches for the object, reads the source, understands the structure, reads class includes, creates the transport, locks the object, writes the code, runs a syntax check, activates, and unlocks. All from one natural language request.

 

Important Hint

It can make ABAP-specific mistakes (like forgetting RAP locking fields, or using reserved DDIC words). Always review AI-generated code before activating in production. But as a development accelerator and code reviewer, it's genuinely powerful.

References

  • GitHub: mcp-abap-adt — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: mcp-abap-abap-adt-api — Mario Andreschak
  • GitHub: sap-skills — 35 Claude Code plugins for SAP
  • Blog: SAP Docs MCP Server — Marian Zeis
  • Blog: Claude Code via MCP — SAP Community
  • Blog: Installing and Extending an ABAP MCP Server — SAP Community

I hope this helps other ABAP developers get started. If you run into issues or have questions, feel free to comment below. Happy coding!

entry.title: Agentic AI for SAP Data Migration: From Assessment to Sign-Off (Series 2, Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 4 OF 6

Agentic AI for SAP Data Migration: From Assessment to Sign-Off

Five migration agents built with LangGraph and SAP AI Core — landscape complexity analysis, data quality validation, runbook generation, execution monitoring, and post-migration reconciliation — covering ECC to S/4HANA, HANA Cloud migration, and general DMLT workstreams.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP data migration projects are among the most complex, high-risk workloads in enterprise IT — and also among the most repetitive. The same assessment questions, the same validation checks, the same runbook structure, the same reconciliation patterns appear in every project. Agentic AI is uniquely suited to this workload because it can plan, execute multi-step tasks, and reason over SAP-specific context autonomously. This article builds five migration agents using LangGraph and SAP AI Core that cover the full migration lifecycle — from initial landscape assessment through post-migration sign-off.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of the Agentic AI in Practice series. Parts 1–3 established the agentic AI architecture, ReAct agent pattern, and multi-agent orchestration on BTP. This part applies those patterns to a domain where agentic AI delivers immediate, measurable value: SAP data migration.

Data migration is the backbone of every SAP transformation — ECC to S/4HANA, on-premise HANA to HANA Cloud, legacy system consolidations. The workstreams are well-defined, the failure modes are known, and the documentation burden is enormous. All three characteristics make migration an ideal first production deployment for SAP AI agents.

WHY DATA MIGRATION IS THE RIGHT STARTING POINT FOR SAP AGENTS 

The Migration Agent Opportunity

Every SAP data migration project runs through the same lifecycle regardless of source system, target system, or migration tool. Assessment, validation, documentation, execution, and reconciliation. The steps are known. The questions are known. The failure patterns are known.

What makes migration painful is not the complexity of individual steps — it's the volume, the manual coordination between steps, and the time pressure that compresses quality at every stage. A consultant who has run ten migrations knows exactly what to check. An agent can apply that same knowledge at the speed and scale that human consultants cannot.

Migration Phase

Current Approach

Typical Time

Agent Advantage

Landscape assessment

Manual review of Z-objects, custom tables, interfaces

2–3 weeks

Agent analyzes in hours with consistent coverage

Data quality validation

Rule-based checks, manual exception review

Ongoing throughout project

Agent applies rules continuously, flags exceptions with context

Runbook generation

Template-based, manually populated per object

1–2 weeks

Agent generates object-specific runbooks from landscape analysis

Execution monitoring

Manual log review, escalation judgment calls

During migration window

Agent monitors in real-time, classifies and escalates autonomously

Reconciliation & sign-off

Manual count comparisons, exception investigation

Post-migration

Agent cross-validates counts, generates sign-off report with exceptions

ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Agent Architecture for SAP Migration Workstreams

The five migration agents in this article share a common architecture pattern. Each agent is implemented as a LangGraph StateGraph — a stateful directed graph where nodes represent reasoning or action steps and edges represent conditional routing based on agent decisions.

SAP Source System → BTP + Cloud Connector → LangGraph Agent → AI Core (GPT-4o) → Output / Action

LangGraph is chosen over simple chain patterns because migration workflows are not linear. An assessment agent may discover a Z-table that requires deeper investigation, loop back, gather more data, and then continue. A monitoring agent may encounter an exception that requires human escalation before proceeding. LangGraph's conditional edges and state management handle these loops and branching decisions cleanly.

The SAP AI Core connection uses the generative-ai-hub-sdk from Series 1. GPT-4o is the recommended model for migration workstreams — its SAP code and schema knowledge consistently outperforms other models for ABAP analysis, table structure interpretation, and technical documentation generation.

All agents in this article connect to SAP systems via BTP Destinations and Cloud Connector — no direct database connections, no ABAP changes required. Read-only access is sufficient for assessment, validation, monitoring, and reconciliation agents. The runbook generation agent requires no SAP connectivity at all — it works from the output of the assessment agent.

AGENT 1 OF 5 

  ▶  Landscape Complexity Assessment Agent 

Agent 1: SAP Landscape Complexity Analyzer

The assessment agent analyzes a source SAP landscape — ECC, S/4HANA, or legacy systems — and produces a structured complexity report covering custom object density, interface dependencies, data volumes, and migration risk indicators. This replaces the manual 2–3 week scoping phase with an agent-driven analysis that completes in hours.

Connect to SAP → Inventory Z-objects → Analyze dependencies → Assess data volumes → Generate risk report
# Agent 1: SAP Landscape Complexity Assessment
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Dict
import requests, json
 
# Define agent state — carries context between nodes
class AssessmentState(TypedDict):
    sap_system:      str    # 'ECC' | 'S4HANA' | 'HANA_CLOUD'
    resource_group:  str
    z_objects:       List[Dict]
    interfaces:      List[Dict]
    data_volumes:    Dict
    risk_indicators: List[str]
    complexity_score: str   # 'LOW' | 'MEDIUM' | 'HIGH' | 'CRITICAL'
    report:          str
    messages:        List
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Node 1: Inventory custom Z-objects via RFC/OData
def inventory_z_objects(state: AssessmentState) -> AssessmentState:
    """
    Query TADIR for custom Z/Y objects — tables, programs, function modules.
    Uses BTP Destination → Cloud Connector → RFC_READ_TABLE or OData.
    """
    # Query via BTP Destination (configured with Cloud Connector for on-prem)
    response = requests.get(
        f"{os.environ['SAP_ODATA_URL']}/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZMT_LANDSCAPE_SRV/ZObjectSet",
        headers={'Authorization': f"Bearer {get_sap_token()}",
                 'Accept': 'application/json'}
    )
    z_objects = response.json().get('d', {}).get('results', [])
    state['z_objects'] = z_objects
    return state
 
# Node 2: AI analyzes Z-objects for migration complexity
def analyze_complexity(state: AssessmentState) -> AssessmentState:
    z_summary = json.dumps(state['z_objects'][:50])  # Top 50 for context
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP migration architect.
        Analyze these custom Z-objects and assess migration complexity.
        Consider: object types, naming patterns, volume, interdependencies.
        Return JSON: {complexity_score, risk_indicators[], key_findings[]}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze Z-objects for {state["sap_system"]}:\n{z_summary}')
    ]
    response = llm(messages)
    analysis = json.loads(response.content)
    state['complexity_score'] = analysis['complexity_score']
    state['risk_indicators'] = analysis['risk_indicators']
    return state
 
# Node 3: Generate structured assessment report
def generate_report(state: AssessmentState) -> AssessmentState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Generate a professional SAP migration
        complexity assessment report. Include executive summary,
        complexity score with justification, top risk items,
        and recommended migration approach.
        Format as structured markdown.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
System: {state['sap_system']}
Z-Objects: {len(state['z_objects'])} custom objects
Complexity: {state['complexity_score']}
Risks: {state['risk_indicators']}''')
    ]
    state['report'] = llm(messages).content
    return state
 
# Build LangGraph
assessment_graph = StateGraph(AssessmentState)
assessment_graph.add_node('inventory', inventory_z_objects)
assessment_graph.add_node('analyze', analyze_complexity)
assessment_graph.add_node('report', generate_report)
assessment_graph.add_edge('inventory', 'analyze')
assessment_graph.add_edge('analyze', 'report')
assessment_graph.add_edge('report', END)
assessment_graph.set_entry_point('inventory')
assessment_agent = assessment_graph.compile()

AGENT 2 OF 5 

  ▶  Data Quality and Mapping Validation Agent 

Agent 2: Data Quality and Mapping Rules Validator

The validation agent applies migration mapping rules to source data records and identifies quality issues — missing mandatory fields, invalid values, orphaned references, duplicate keys — before migration execution. It uses LangGraph's conditional edges to loop back on ambiguous records rather than failing fast.

Load mapping rules → Sample source data → Apply rules → Ambiguous? → AI resolves → Quality report
# Agent 2: Data Quality and Mapping Validation
class ValidationState(TypedDict):
    object_type:     str    # e.g. 'CUSTOMER', 'MATERIAL', 'VENDOR'
    mapping_rules:   List[Dict]
    source_records:  List[Dict]
    failed_records:  List[Dict]
    ambiguous_records: List[Dict]
    validation_pass: bool
    quality_report:  str
 
# Node: Apply mapping rules — flag failed and ambiguous records
def apply_mapping_rules(state: ValidationState) -> ValidationState:
    failed, ambiguous = [], []
    for record in state['source_records']:
        issues = []
        for rule in state['mapping_rules']:
            field = rule['source_field']
            if rule['mandatory'] and not record.get(field):
                issues.append(f"Missing mandatory field: {field}")
            if rule.get('valid_values') and record.get(field) not in rule['valid_values']:
                if rule.get('allow_ai_resolution'):
                    ambiguous.append({'record': record, 'issue': issues})
                else:
                    failed.append({'record': record, 'issues': issues})
    state['failed_records'] = failed
    state['ambiguous_records'] = ambiguous
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: route ambiguous records to AI resolution
def route_validation(state: ValidationState) -> str:
    if state['ambiguous_records']:
        return 'resolve_ambiguous'
    return 'generate_quality_report'
 
# Node: AI resolves ambiguous mapping decisions
def resolve_ambiguous(state: ValidationState) -> ValidationState:
    for item in state['ambiguous_records']:
        messages = [
            SystemMessage(content=f'''You are an SAP {state['object_type']}
            master data expert. Resolve this mapping ambiguity.
            Return JSON: {{resolution, confidence, target_value}}'''),
            HumanMessage(content=f'Record: {json.dumps(item)}')
        ]
        resolution = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
        if resolution['confidence'] == 'LOW':
            state['failed_records'].append({**item, 'resolution': resolution})
    state['ambiguous_records'] = []
    return state

The conditional edge pattern is the key architectural advantage of LangGraph for migration validation. Simple chain patterns fail fast on ambiguous records. The LangGraph approach routes them to AI resolution first — only escalating to failed status when confidence is genuinely low. This significantly reduces false failure rates in migration validation runs.

AGENT 3 OF 5 

  ▶  Migration Runbook Generator Agent 

Agent 3: Migration Runbook and Documentation Generator

The runbook agent takes the assessment report from Agent 1 and generates object-specific migration runbooks — step-by-step execution guides, rollback procedures, and sign-off checklists — tailored to the specific objects, volumes, and risk profile of the landscape being migrated. This replaces the most time-consuming documentation phase of any migration project.

Assessment report → Identify migration objects → Generate per-object runbook → Generate rollback plan → Compile full runbook
# Agent 3: Migration Runbook Generator
class RunbookState(TypedDict):
    assessment_report: str
    migration_type:    str  # 'ECC_TO_S4' | 'HANA_CLOUD' | 'DMLT'
    migration_objects: List[str]
    object_runbooks:   Dict[str, str]
    rollback_plan:     str
    full_runbook:      str
 
def extract_migration_objects(state: RunbookState) -> RunbookState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Extract the list of SAP objects
        requiring migration runbooks from this assessment report.
        Return JSON array of object names only.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=state['assessment_report'])
    ]
    state['migration_objects'] = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    return state
 
def generate_object_runbooks(state: RunbookState) -> RunbookState:
    runbooks = {}
    for obj in state['migration_objects']:
        messages = [
            SystemMessage(content=f'''Generate a detailed SAP migration
            runbook for this object in a {state['migration_type']} scenario.
            Include: pre-checks, extraction steps, transformation rules,
            load sequence, validation steps, rollback trigger criteria.
            Format as numbered steps with time estimates.'''),
            HumanMessage(content=f'Object: {obj}\nContext: {state["assessment_report"][:500]}')
        ]
        runbooks[obj] = llm(messages).content
    state['object_runbooks'] = runbooks
    return state
 
def generate_rollback_plan(state: RunbookState) -> RunbookState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Generate a comprehensive rollback plan
        for this SAP migration. Include: rollback triggers, sequence,
        data restoration steps, system validation after rollback,
        and communication plan. Be specific to the migration type.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Migration: {state["migration_type"]}\nObjects: {state["migration_objects"]}')
    ]
    state['rollback_plan'] = llm(messages).content
    return state

Runbook quality is directly proportional to assessment report quality. The runbook agent generates object-specific procedures from the assessment context — vague assessment outputs produce generic runbooks. Run Agent 1 thoroughly before triggering Agent 3 for production migration documentation.

AGENT 4 OF 5 

  ▶  Migration Execution Monitor Agent 

Agent 4: Real-Time Migration Execution Monitor

The monitoring agent watches migration execution logs in real time, classifies exceptions by severity and type, determines whether exceptions require immediate human escalation or can continue, and generates a running status report for the migration team. It uses LangGraph's loop pattern to continuously process new log entries throughout the migration window.

Poll migration logs → Parse new entries → Classify exceptions → Escalate? → Continue or halt → Update status
# Agent 4: Migration Execution Monitor
class MonitorState(TypedDict):
    log_source:         str   # log file path or API endpoint
    last_processed_line: int
    exceptions:         List[Dict]
    critical_flags:     List[str]
    escalation_required: bool
    migration_status:   str   # 'RUNNING' | 'WARNING' | 'CRITICAL' | 'COMPLETE'
    status_report:      str
 
def poll_migration_logs(state: MonitorState) -> MonitorState:
    """Read new log entries since last processed position."""
    with open(state['log_source'], 'r') as f:
        lines = f.readlines()
    new_lines = lines[state['last_processed_line']:]
    state['last_processed_line'] = len(lines)
    # Parse new entries — store as raw text for AI classification
    state['_new_log_entries'] = '\n'.join(new_lines[-100:])  # Last 100 lines
    return state
 
def classify_exceptions(state: MonitorState) -> MonitorState:
    if not state.get('_new_log_entries'):
        return state
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP migration expert.
        Classify exceptions from these migration logs.
        Return JSON: {exceptions: [{type, severity, message, action}],
        overall_status: RUNNING|WARNING|CRITICAL,
        escalation_required: bool,
        escalation_reason: str}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Migration logs:\n{state["_new_log_entries"]}')
    ]
    result = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    state['exceptions'].extend(result['exceptions'])
    state['migration_status'] = result['overall_status']
    state['escalation_required'] = result['escalation_required']
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: escalate critical issues, loop for non-critical
def route_monitoring(state: MonitorState) -> str:
    if state['migration_status'] == 'CRITICAL':
        return 'escalate'
    if state['migration_status'] == 'COMPLETE':
        return 'final_report'
    return 'poll_migration_logs'  # Continue monitoring loop

AGENT 5 OF 5 

  ▶  Post-Migration Reconciliation and Sign-Off Agent 

Agent 5: Post-Migration Reconciliation and Sign-Off

The reconciliation agent performs post-migration validation — comparing source and target record counts, validating key field mappings, identifying orphaned records, and generating a sign-off report with exception summary. This is the final gate before a migration is declared complete.

Source counts → Target counts → Cross-validate → Investigate gaps → Generate sign-off report
# Agent 5: Post-Migration Reconciliation
class ReconciliationState(TypedDict):
    migration_objects:  List[str]
    source_counts:      Dict[str, int]
    target_counts:      Dict[str, int]
    discrepancies:      List[Dict]
    investigated_gaps:  List[Dict]
    sign_off_status:    str   # 'APPROVED' | 'CONDITIONAL' | 'REJECTED'
    sign_off_report:    str
 
def fetch_source_counts(state: ReconciliationState) -> ReconciliationState:
    counts = {}
    for obj in state['migration_objects']:
        # Query source system record counts via BTP Destination
        response = requests.get(
            f"{os.environ['SOURCE_ODATA_URL']}/{obj}Set/$count",
            headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {get_sap_token()}'}
        )
        counts[obj] = int(response.text)
    state['source_counts'] = counts
    return state
 
def cross_validate_counts(state: ReconciliationState) -> ReconciliationState:
    discrepancies = []
    for obj in state['migration_objects']:
        src=state['source_counts'].get(obj, 0)
        tgt = state['target_counts'].get(obj, 0)
        variance_pct = abs(src - tgt) / src * 100 if src > 0 else 0
        if variance_pct > 0.01:  # Flag >0.01% variance
            discrepancies.append({
                'object': obj, 'source': src, 'target': tgt,
                'variance_pct': round(variance_pct, 4),
                'delta': src - tgt
            })
    state['discrepancies'] = discrepancies
    return state
 
def generate_sign_off_report(state: ReconciliationState) -> ReconciliationState:
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP migration lead.
        Generate a post-migration sign-off report.
        Determine sign-off status: APPROVED (no significant gaps),
        CONDITIONAL (minor gaps with accepted risk), REJECTED (critical gaps).
        Include executive summary, object-level results, exception table,
        and recommendation. Format professionally for stakeholder review.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
Migration objects: {state['migration_objects']}
Source counts: {state['source_counts']}
Target counts: {state['target_counts']}
Discrepancies: {state['discrepancies']}
Investigated gaps: {state['investigated_gaps']}''')
    ]
    response = llm(messages).content
    # Extract sign-off status from response
    status = 'APPROVED' if 'APPROVED' in response else
             'CONDITIONAL' if 'CONDITIONAL' in response else 'REJECTED'
    state['sign_off_status'] = status
    state['sign_off_report'] = response
    return state

The sign-off report generated by Agent 5 is production-ready for stakeholder review. It includes the variance analysis, exception table, risk assessment, and go/no-go recommendation in a format that migration leads and project managers can use directly — reducing the post-migration documentation effort from days to minutes.

ORCHESTRATING ALL FIVE AGENTS 

Running the Full Migration Agent Pipeline

The five agents are designed to run sequentially with each agent's output feeding the next. This is the complete migration lifecycle orchestration:

# Full migration agent pipeline orchestration
def run_migration_agent_pipeline(sap_system, migration_type, log_source):
    print('Phase 1: Landscape Assessment...')
    assessment_result = assessment_agent.invoke({
        'sap_system': sap_system,
        'resource_group': 'default',
        'z_objects': [], 'interfaces': [], 'data_volumes': {},
        'risk_indicators': [], 'complexity_score': '', 'report': '', 'messages': []
    })
 
    print('Phase 2: Runbook Generation...')
    runbook_result = runbook_agent.invoke({
        'assessment_report': assessment_result['report'],
        'migration_type': migration_type,
        'migration_objects': [], 'object_runbooks': {},
        'rollback_plan': '', 'full_runbook': ''
    })
 
    print('Phase 3: Data Quality Validation...')
    # Run validation agent per object type
 
    print('Phase 4: Migration Execution Monitoring...')
    monitor_result = monitor_agent.invoke({
        'log_source': log_source,
        'last_processed_line': 0,
        'exceptions': [], 'critical_flags': [],
        'escalation_required': False,
        'migration_status': 'RUNNING', 'status_report': ''
    })
 
    print('Phase 5: Post-Migration Reconciliation...')
    recon_result = recon_agent.invoke({
        'migration_objects': runbook_result['migration_objects'],
        'source_counts': {}, 'target_counts': {},
        'discrepancies': [], 'investigated_gaps': [],
        'sign_off_status': '', 'sign_off_report': ''
    })
 
    return {
        'assessment':    assessment_result['report'],
        'runbook':       runbook_result['full_runbook'],
        'monitoring':    monitor_result['status_report'],
        'sign_off':      recon_result['sign_off_report'],
        'sign_off_status': recon_result['sign_off_status']
    }

DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

What Makes Migration Agents Production-Ready

 

Principle

Implementation Guidance

Human-in-the-loop on critical decisions

Agents flag — humans decide on CRITICAL exceptions, low-confidence resolutions, and REJECTED sign-offs. Never fully autonomous on irreversible migration actions.

Audit trail on every agent action

Every agent node logs its inputs, outputs, and AI reasoning to HANA Cloud. Migration auditors need to trace every automated decision post-go-live.

Read-only SAP connectivity

All five agents read from SAP systems — none write back. Writing is performed by the migration tool (DMLT, LTMC, etc.) after human review of agent outputs.

Graceful degradation

If an agent node fails, the pipeline logs the failure and produces a partial output rather than crashing. Incomplete outputs are more useful than no output.

Model selection per agent

GPT-4o for code analysis (Agent 1), validation logic (Agent 2), and log classification (Agent 4). Claude for documentation generation (Agents 3 and 5) where structured long-form output quality matters.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents — approval workflows, escalation patterns, and audit trail architecture for production agentic SAP systems
  • Part 6: Production Agentic SAP Systems — monitoring, observability, failure handling, and cost management for agents running at enterprise scale

The five agents in this article represent the most immediate ROI opportunity for agentic AI in SAP — the migration workstream is well-defined, the failure modes are known, and the documentation burden is enormous. If you implement any of these agents in your migration practice, share your results in the comments. The community variations are where the most valuable refinements emerge.

entry.title: Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit (Series 2, Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 5 OF 6

Human-in-the-Loop SAP Agents: Approval, Escalation, and Audit

Five production patterns for keeping humans in control of agentic SAP systems — approval workflows using LangGraph interrupt(), confidence-based routing, smart escalation, timeout and fallback handling, and audit trail architecture — built on SAP migration and S/4HANA change request use cases.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 5 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI systems that operate autonomously in SAP landscapes must have structured human checkpoints — not as a limitation, but as the architecture that makes them trustworthy enough to deploy in production. This article covers five human-in-the-loop patterns: approval workflows using LangGraph's interrupt() mechanism, confidence-based autonomous vs. escalation routing, smart escalation to the right person by exception type, timeout and fallback handling when humans don't respond, and audit trail architecture that satisfies enterprise compliance requirements. Two SAP use cases anchor the examples: migration sign-off approval from Part 4 and S/4HANA change request approval.

Series Context

This is Part 5 of the Agentic AI in Practice series. Part 4 established the principle — agents flag, humans decide. This part implements that principle in production code across five patterns with two real SAP use cases.

The patterns here apply to any agentic SAP workload — not just migration. Once you understand how LangGraph's interrupt mechanism works with Teams notifications and audit logging, you can apply it to procurement approvals, change request reviews, exception handling in any business process.

WHY HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP IS AN ARCHITECTURE DECISION, NOT A FEATURE 

The Case for Structured Human Checkpoints

The temptation with agentic systems is to minimize human touchpoints — the less interruption, the more automation value. This is the wrong frame for SAP contexts.

SAP systems are systems of record. Actions taken in them — purchase orders raised, materials deactivated, migration objects loaded — have financial, operational, and compliance consequences that cannot be easily reversed. An agent that acts autonomously in these systems without structured human checkpoints is not more capable. It is more dangerous.

Human-in-the-loop is not about limiting what agents can do. It is about defining precisely which decisions require human judgment and ensuring those decisions always reach the right person with the right context at the right time.

Pattern

What It Does

SAP Example

Approval workflow

Agent pauses at a defined checkpoint and waits for explicit human approval before proceeding

Migration sign-off, change request authorization

Confidence-based routing

Agent decides autonomously when confidence is HIGH, escalates when MEDIUM or LOW

Data quality resolution, mapping ambiguity

Smart escalation

Agent routes to the correct person based on exception type and severity

Critical migration errors, compliance violations

Timeout and fallback

Agent handles non-response with defined fallback behavior

Approval delays during migration windows

Audit trail

Every agent decision is logged with inputs, reasoning, and outcome for compliance

All production agentic workloads

PATTERN 1: APPROVAL WORKFLOW WITH LANGGRAPH INTERRUPT() 

  ▶  LangGraph interrupt() — Agent Pauses for Human Approval 

Pattern 1: Approval Workflow

LangGraph's interrupt() function pauses graph execution at a defined node, persists the current state to a checkpointer, and waits for human input before resuming. This is the correct mechanism for approval workflows — the agent's work is preserved, the human reviews the agent's output and decision, and execution continues from exactly where it paused.

Agent completes work → interrupt() — pause → Human reviews → Approve / Reject → Resume or terminate
# Pattern 1: Approval workflow using LangGraph interrupt()
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from langgraph.types import interrupt, Command
from typing import TypedDict, Literal
 
class MigrationApprovalState(TypedDict):
    sign_off_report:   str
    sign_off_status:   str    # APPROVED | CONDITIONAL | REJECTED
    discrepancies:     list
    human_decision:    str    # 'approve' | 'reject' | 'conditional'
    human_comments:    str
    final_status:      str
    audit_log:         list
 
# Node: Agent prepares sign-off package for human review
def prepare_sign_off(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    # Agent has already generated sign-off report (from Part 4 Agent 5)
    # Log the preparation step
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'SIGN_OFF_PREPARED',
        'agent_recommendation': state['sign_off_status'],
        'discrepancy_count': len(state['discrepancies'])
    })
    return state
 
# Node: interrupt() pauses execution and waits for human decision
def request_human_approval(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    """
    interrupt() suspends the graph here.
    State is persisted to checkpointer.
    Execution resumes when resume() is called with human decision.
    """
    human_input = interrupt({
        'message': 'Migration sign-off requires your approval.',
        'agent_recommendation': state['sign_off_status'],
        'report_summary': state['sign_off_report'][:500],
        'discrepancies': state['discrepancies'],
        'options': ['approve', 'conditional', 'reject']
    })
    state['human_decision'] = human_input['decision']
    state['human_comments'] = human_input.get('comments', '')
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'HUMAN_DECISION',
        'decision': human_input['decision'],
        'reviewer': human_input.get('reviewer', 'unknown'),
        'comments': human_input.get('comments', '')
    })
    return state
 
# Node: process outcome based on human decision
def process_approval_decision(state: MigrationApprovalState) -> MigrationApprovalState:
    decision = state['human_decision']
    if decision == 'approve':
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_APPROVED'
    elif decision == 'conditional':
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_APPROVED_WITH_CONDITIONS'
    else:
        state['final_status'] = 'MIGRATION_REJECTED'
    state['audit_log'].append({
        'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat(),
        'action': 'FINAL_STATUS_SET',
        'status': state['final_status']
    })
    return state
 
# Build graph with MemorySaver checkpointer — required for interrupt()
checkpointer = MemorySaver()
approval_graph = StateGraph(MigrationApprovalState)
approval_graph.add_node('prepare', prepare_sign_off)
approval_graph.add_node('request_approval', request_human_approval)
approval_graph.add_node('process_decision', process_approval_decision)
approval_graph.add_edge('prepare', 'request_approval')
approval_graph.add_edge('request_approval', 'process_decision')
approval_graph.add_edge('process_decision', END)
approval_graph.set_entry_point('prepare')
approval_agent = approval_graph.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer, interrupt_before=['request_approval'])
 
# Resume after human provides decision
def resume_with_human_decision(thread_id, decision, reviewer, comments=''):
    config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': thread_id}}
    approval_agent.invoke(
        Command(resume={'decision': decision, 'reviewer': reviewer, 'comments': comments}),
        config=confi

A checkpointer is mandatory for interrupt() to work. Without it, graph state is not persisted and the interrupt cannot be resumed. MemorySaver works for development and testing. For production, use a database-backed checkpointer (PostgreSQL or HANA Cloud) so approval state survives application restarts.

PATTERN 2: CONFIDENCE-BASED ROUTING 

  ▶  Agent decides autonomously when confident — escalates when not 

Pattern 2: Confidence-Based Autonomous vs. Escalation Routing

Not every agent decision requires human review. Confidence-based routing lets the agent act autonomously on HIGH confidence decisions while escalating MEDIUM and LOW confidence cases. This maximizes automation value while preserving human oversight where it actually matters.

Agent decision → Assess confidence → HIGH → autonomous → MEDIUM/LOW → escalate
# Pattern 2: Confidence-based routing for S/4HANA change request
class ChangeRequestState(TypedDict):
    change_request:    dict
    risk_assessment:   dict
    confidence:        str    # 'HIGH' | 'MEDIUM' | 'LOW'
    auto_approved:     bool
    escalation_target: str
    decision:          str
    audit_log:         list
 
def assess_change_risk(state: ChangeRequestState) -> ChangeRequestState:
    """Agent analyzes change request and assigns confidence level."""
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content='''Assess this S/4HANA change request.
        Return JSON: {risk_level: LOW|MEDIUM|HIGH|CRITICAL,
        confidence: HIGH|MEDIUM|LOW,
        auto_approve_eligible: bool,
        reasoning: str,
        escalation_role: str}
        AUTO_APPROVE only if: LOW risk, HIGH confidence, no financial impact > 10k,
        no core customizing changes, standard transport route.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Change request: {json.dumps(state["change_request"])}')
    ]
    result = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    state['risk_assessment'] = result
    state['confidence'] = result['confidence']
    state['auto_approved'] = result['auto_approve_eligible'] and result['confidence'] == 'HIGH'
    state['escalation_target'] = result.get('escalation_role', 'change_manager')
    return state
 
# Conditional edge: route based on confidence and auto-approval eligibility
def route_by_confidence(state: ChangeRequestState) -> str:
    if state['auto_approved']:
        return 'auto_approve'
    elif state['confidence'] == 'MEDIUM':
        return 'request_review'    # Human review but not full approval flow
    else:
        return 'escalate'          # Full escalation to change manager

PATTERN 3: SMART ESCALATION 

  ▶  Route exceptions to the right person based on type and severity 

Pattern 3: Smart Escalation Routing

Not all exceptions should go to the same person. A financial variance in migration reconciliation goes to the Finance Lead. A technical ABAP error goes to the Basis team. A security-related change request goes to the GRC team. Smart escalation uses AI to classify exceptions and route them to the correct stakeholder — with full context, not just an alert.

# Pattern 3: Smart escalation with role-based routing
ESCALATION_MATRIX = {
    'FINANCIAL_VARIANCE':   {'role': 'finance_lead',    'sla_hours': 2},
    'TECHNICAL_ERROR':      {'role': 'basis_team',      'sla_hours': 4},
    'DATA_QUALITY':         {'role': 'data_owner',      'sla_hours': 8},
    'SECURITY_VIOLATION':   {'role': 'grc_team',        'sla_hours': 1},
    'COMPLIANCE_RISK':      {'role': 'compliance_lead', 'sla_hours': 2},
    'PERFORMANCE_ISSUE':    {'role': 'basis_team',      'sla_hours': 4},
}
 
def smart_escalate(state, exception_text: str, context: dict) -> dict:
    """Classify exception and route to correct stakeholder via Teams + Email."""
    # Step 1: Classify exception type
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify this SAP exception.
        Return JSON: {{exception_type, severity, affected_area, recommended_action}}
        Exception types: {list(ESCALATION_MATRIX.keys())}'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Exception: {exception_text}\nContext: {json.dumps(context)}')
    ]
    classification = json.loads(llm(messages).content)
    exception_type = classification['exception_type']
 
    # Step 2: Look up escalation target and SLA
    escalation = ESCALATION_MATRIX.get(exception_type,
                 {'role': 'change_manager', 'sla_hours': 4})
 
    # Step 3: Generate context-rich escalation message via Claude
    msg_response = claude_client.messages.create(
        model='claude-sonnet-4-20250514', max_tokens=400,
        messages=[{'role': 'user', 'content': f'''
Write a concise escalation notification for {escalation['role']}.
Exception: {exception_text}
Classification: {json.dumps(classification)}
SLA: {escalation['sla_hours']} hours. Include: what happened,
impact, recommended action, urgency. Professional tone.'''}]
    )
    escalation_message = msg_response.content[0].text
 
    # Step 4: Deliver via Teams and email
    recipient = get_role_contact(escalation['role'])
    send_teams_message(ms_token, recipient['email'], escalation_message)
    send_email(ms_token, recipient['email'],
               f"SAP Exception Escalation: {exception_type}", escalation_message)
 
    return {'type': exception_type, 'routed_to': escalation['role'],
            'sla_hours': escalation['sla_hours'], 'message': escalation_message}

PATTERN 4: TIMEOUT AND FALLBACK HANDLING 

  ▶  What happens when humans don't respond 

Pattern 4: Timeout and Fallback Handling

Migration windows are time-constrained. If an agent requests human approval at 2am and the approver doesn't respond within the SLA window, the agent needs a defined fallback behavior — not a silent hang. Three fallback strategies cover most SAP scenarios:

  • Escalate up — notify the next level in the approval chain
  • Safe halt — pause the migration, preserve state, alert all stakeholders
  • Proceed with logging — continue autonomously for LOW risk decisions, log prominently
# Pattern 4: Timeout handling with fallback strategies
import asyncio
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
 
async def request_approval_with_timeout(
    approval_request: dict,
    timeout_hours: float = 2.0,
    fallback_strategy: str = 'escalate_up'  # 'escalate_up'|'safe_halt'|'proceed'
) -> dict:
    """
    Request human approval with configurable timeout and fallback.
    Sends initial notification then polls for response.
    """
    deadline = datetime.now() + timedelta(hours=timeout_hours)
    thread_id = approval_request['thread_id']
 
    # Send initial approval request
    await send_approval_notification(approval_request)
 
    # Poll for response until deadline
    while datetime.now() < deadline:
        response = check_approval_response(thread_id)
        if response:
            return {'status': 'RESPONDED', 'decision': response}
 
        # Send reminder at 50% of timeout window
        elapsed = (datetime.now() - (deadline - timedelta(hours=timeout_hours))).total_seconds()
        if elapsed > timeout_hours * 3600 * 0.5 and not approval_request.get('reminder_sent'):
            await send_reminder_notification(approval_request)
            approval_request['reminder_sent'] = True
 
        await asyncio.sleep(300)  # Poll every 5 minutes
 
    # Timeout reached — apply fallback strategy
    return await apply_fallback(approval_request, fallback_strategy)
 
async def apply_fallback(request: dict, strategy: str) -> dict:
    if strategy == 'escalate_up':
        # Notify next level in approval chain
        escalation_target = get_next_approver(request['requested_from'])
        await send_escalation_notification(request, escalation_target)
        return {'status': 'ESCALATED', 'escalated_to': escalation_target}
 
    elif strategy == 'safe_halt':
        # Halt migration, preserve state, alert all stakeholders
        await notify_all_stakeholders(request, 'MIGRATION_HALTED_APPROVAL_TIMEOUT')
        return {'status': 'HALTED', 'reason': 'APPROVAL_TIMEOUT'}
 
    elif strategy == 'proceed':
        # Proceed autonomously — only for explicitly LOW risk decisions
        log_autonomous_proceed(request)
        return {'status': 'PROCEEDED_AUTONOMOUSLY', 'risk_accepted': True}

The 'proceed' fallback strategy should only be configured for explicitly LOW risk, fully reversible decisions. Never use 'proceed' as a fallback for migration execution, financial postings, or any action that cannot be undone. Document every instance of autonomous proceed in the audit trail with the risk acceptance rationale.

PATTERN 5: AUDIT TRAIL ARCHITECTURE 

  ▶  Every agent decision logged for compliance 

Pattern 5: Audit Trail Architecture

Enterprise compliance requirements for agentic SAP systems are not the same as application logging. An audit trail must capture not just what happened but why — what the agent's inputs were, what reasoning it applied, what it decided, and what the human review outcome was. This is what auditors and compliance teams need to validate that AI-assisted SAP decisions were appropriate.

# Pattern 5: Comprehensive audit trail for agentic SAP decisions
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from datetime import datetime
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
 
@dataclass
class AgentAuditEntry:
    entry_id:          str
    timestamp:         str
    session_id:        str    # Links all entries for one agent run
    agent_name:        str
    node_name:         str    # Which LangGraph node
    action_type:       str    # AUTONOMOUS | ESCALATED | APPROVED | REJECTED | TIMEOUT
    input_summary:     str    # Truncated inputs (no sensitive data)
    ai_reasoning:      str    # Model's reasoning for the decision
    ai_confidence:     str    # HIGH | MEDIUM | LOW
    decision:          str    # What was decided
    human_reviewer:    str    # Email if human was involved
    human_decision:    str    # Human's decision if applicable
    human_comments:    str
    sap_objects:       str    # Affected SAP objects (JSON)
    compliance_flags:  str    # Any compliance considerations flagged
 
class AgentAuditLogger:
    def __init__(self, hana_conn):
        self.conn = hana_conn
        self._ensure_audit_table()
 
    def _ensure_audit_table(self):
        self.conn.cursor().execute('''
            CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS (
                ENTRY_ID        NVARCHAR(100) PRIMARY KEY,
                TIMESTAMP       TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
                SESSION_ID      NVARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
                AGENT_NAME      NVARCHAR(100),
                NODE_NAME       NVARCHAR(100),
                ACTION_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50),
                INPUT_SUMMARY   NCLOB,
                AI_REASONING    NCLOB,
                AI_CONFIDENCE   NVARCHAR(20),
                DECISION        NCLOB,
                HUMAN_REVIEWER  NVARCHAR(200),
                HUMAN_DECISION  NVARCHAR(100),
                HUMAN_COMMENTS  NCLOB,
                SAP_OBJECTS     NCLOB,
                COMPLIANCE_FLAGS NCLOB
            )
        ''')
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def log(self, entry: AgentAuditEntry):
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS VALUES
            (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)
        ''', list(asdict(entry).values()))
        self.conn.commit()
 
    def get_session_trail(self, session_id: str) -> list:
        """Retrieve complete audit trail for one agent run — for compliance review."""
        cursor = self.conn.cursor()
        cursor.execute('''
            SELECT * FROM AI_AUDIT.AGENT_DECISIONS
            WHERE SESSION_ID = ?
            ORDER BY TIMESTAMP ASC
        ''', [session_id])
        return cursor.fetchall()

Store audit entries in HANA Cloud — the same instance used for the vector store in Part 4. This keeps all AI-related data in one governed location with the same access controls and backup policies as your SAP operational data. Audit entries should be treated as immutable — no updates, no deletes. Append only.

MICROSOFT TEAMS INTEGRATION FOR APPROVAL NOTIFICATIONS 

Delivering Approval Requests via Microsoft Teams

The interrupt() pattern pauses the agent, but humans still need to be notified and need a way to respond. Microsoft Graph API delivers the approval request as a Teams message and captures the response. This connects the LangGraph approval flow to the communication channel SAP practitioners already use.

# Teams notification for approval request
def send_approval_request_teams(ms_token: str, approver_email: str,
                                approval_data: dict, thread_id: str):
    """
    Send structured approval request to approver via Teams.
    Includes agent recommendation, key data, and response instructions.
    """
    message_body = f'''
SAP Migration Sign-Off Request
================================
Agent Recommendation: {approval_data['agent_recommendation']}
Discrepancies Found: {approval_data['discrepancy_count']}
Migration Objects: {', '.join(approval_data['migration_objects'])}
 
Summary:
{approval_data['report_summary']}
 
To respond, reply to this message with:
APPROVE - to approve migration sign-off
CONDITIONAL: [your conditions] - to approve with conditions
REJECT: [reason] - to reject
 
Thread ID: {thread_id} (required in your response)
Response required within 2 hours.
    '''
    send_teams_message(ms_token, approver_email, message_body,
                       subject='Action Required: SAP Migration Sign-Off')

COMPLETE HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP FLOW 

S/4HANA Change Request: Complete Human-in-the-Loop Flow

This example combines all five patterns — confidence routing, smart escalation, approval workflow, timeout handling, and audit trail — into a single change request processing flow:

CR received → Assess risk + confidence → HIGH: auto-approve → LOW/MEDIUM: request approval → Timeout? Escalate up → Audit all steps
# Complete HITL flow for S/4HANA change request processing
async def process_change_request_with_hitl(change_request: dict) -> dict:
    session_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    audit_logger = AgentAuditLogger(hana_conn)
 
    # Step 1: Assess risk and confidence
    assessment = assess_change_risk({'change_request': change_request, ...})
    audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(
        entry_id=str(uuid.uuid4()), session_id=session_id,
        agent_name='ChangeRequestAgent', node_name='assess_risk',
        action_type='AUTONOMOUS',
        ai_reasoning=assessment['risk_assessment']['reasoning'],
        ai_confidence=assessment['confidence'],
        decision=f"Risk: {assessment['risk_assessment']['risk_level']}",
        ...
    ))
 
    # Step 2: Route based on confidence
    if assessment['auto_approved']:
        audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(action_type='AUTONOMOUS', ...))
        return {'status': 'AUTO_APPROVED', 'session_id': session_id}
 
    # Step 3: Request human approval with timeout
    approval_result = await request_approval_with_timeout(
        approval_request={
            'thread_id': session_id,
            'requested_from': assessment['escalation_target'],
            'change_request': change_request,
            'risk_assessment': assessment['risk_assessment']
        },
        timeout_hours=2.0,
        fallback_strategy='escalate_up'
    )
 
    # Step 4: Log final outcome
    audit_logger.log(AgentAuditEntry(
        action_type=approval_result['status'],
        human_reviewer=approval_result.get('reviewer', ''),
        human_decision=approval_result.get('decision', ''),
        ...
    ))
 
    return {'status': approval_result['status'], 'session_id': session_id}

PRODUCTION DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

Human-in-the-Loop Design Principles for SAP Agents

Principle

Implementation Guidance

Define human checkpoints before writing code

Identify which decisions require human oversight before building the agent. Adding checkpoints retroactively is significantly harder than designing them in from the start.

Use database checkpointers in production

MemorySaver loses state on restart. Production approval workflows must survive application restarts — use PostgreSQL or HANA Cloud-backed checkpointers.

Never use 'proceed' fallback for irreversible actions

Autonomous proceed is only acceptable for LOW risk, fully reversible decisions. Document every instance explicitly in the audit trail with risk acceptance rationale.

Audit trail is not optional

Every agentic decision in a production SAP system needs an auditable record. Compliance teams will ask for it. Build it from day one, not as an afterthought.

SLA-driven timeout escalation

Every approval request needs a timeout and fallback. Define SLAs by exception type — financial variances get 2 hours, data quality issues get 8. Match the urgency to the business impact.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 6: Production Agentic SAP Systems — monitoring, observability, cost management, failure handling, and scaling agentic workloads at enterprise SAP scale

The patterns in this article — interrupt(), confidence routing, smart escalation, timeout handling, and audit trail — are the production readiness layer that separates agentic SAP proofs of concept from systems organizations can actually trust and deploy. If you implement any of these patterns and find edge cases worth documenting, share them in the comments.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition - The “Trust Layer” for Agentic AI
entry.content:
For the last year, the world has been captivated by AI’s ability to answer. The next era belongs to AI that can act.

Agentic AI promises something bigger than copilots and chat interfaces: a digital workforce that can sense what’s happening, reason over context, and initiate the next best action across finance, supply chain, HR, procurement, and customer operations. But here’s the reality most enterprises discover quickly: the leap from clever prototype to production-grade agent isn’t a model problem — it’s a trust problem.

Trust is earned in the connective tissue of the enterprise: identity, governance, reliable integration, observability, and controlled execution across SAP and non‑SAP systems. That’s exactly why we’re excited to announce SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition — designed to help organizations move from AI experimentation to agentic outcomes by providing an integration foundation where agents can operate safely, securely, and at scale.

From “AI that knows” to “AI that does”: why integration becomes strategic

Agentic AI doesn’t live in a single application. It lives between applications — where approvals happen, where exceptions are resolved, where events trigger downstream actions, where APIs enforce guardrails, and where process continuity must be protected.

This is the moment when integration becomes more than plumbing. It becomes the execution fabric for AI.

SAP’s vision for Integration Suite is explicit: unify your enterprise with secure, AI‑ready integration and the governance required to scale agentic AI across the business — not as an add-on, but as an enterprise capability.

What’s new in enhanced edition — and why it’s built for agentic reality

SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition provides access to newly released AI capabilities that focus on two things agentic AI needs most:
