  • BTP specialist: BTP Core Services via its MCP server
  • Audit log specialist: scaffolded and disabled while I iterate on scopes

Each specialist wraps an MCP client that speaks Streamable HTTP to its MCP server, authorised with OAuth 2.1. A small shared module (agents/shared.py) centralises the AI Core GenAI Hub model setup, the MCP client factory and the OAuth flow, so every specialist is stamped from the same template. A legacy agent.py sits next to the new agents/ package as a reference to the single‑agent design from the previous post, useful when explaining the migration.

Key configuration patterns

The app supports two credential layouts for AI Core so it runs comfortably both locally and on CF:

  • Individual environment variables — AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET, AICORE_BASE_URL, AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP.
  • A single AICORE_SERVICE_KEY JSON — the whole service key dropped in as one variable, handy on CF where a service binding gives you exactly that shape.

MCP server URLs are injected the same way: MCP_CLOUDFOUNDRY_URL, MCP_BTP_URL and MCP_AUDITLOG_URL. Locally you get them by running the MCP servers on 127.0.0.1; on CF the mta.yaml descriptor wires them to the deployed hosts. The AI Core binding is attached automatically so you don’t have to ship credentials into the image.

 

What the orchestrator actually does

The orchestrator is deliberately small. Its prompt tells it: you are a BTP operations assistant; you have the following specialists available; when a user asks a question, decide which specialist(s) can answer, call them, and combine their output into a single response. Under the hood, each delegation tool invokes the corresponding specialist agent with a sub‑prompt, collects the result and returns it to the orchestrator. That lets the model reason at two levels (routing at the top, tool selection inside each specialist) which is exactly what the single‑agent design was trying and failing to do in one go.

The outcome is measurable. Tool lists per agent drop from "well over a hundred" to "a dozen or two", token usage per turn drops with them and the agent stops hallucinating tool names because each specialist only knows the tools that actually make sense for its domain. Debugging also gets easier: when something goes wrong, you can tell whether the orchestrator mis‑routed or the specialist mis‑used a tool, just by reading the trace.

 

What I learned (and the OAuth honesty section)

The multi‑agent pattern pays for itself instantly. Tool lists per agent drop from "well over a hundred" to "a dozen or two", token usage per turn drops with them, no tool-overload and the model stops hallucinating tool names because each specialist only sees the tools that make sense for its domain. OAuth 2.1 against the MCP servers also works fine once deployed, Pydantic AI ships a web client that handles the consent flow inside the running app, so the same code path that works locally works on Cloud Foundry.

What this iteration did make obvious is that the list of specialists is still compiled into the code. Add a sixth MCP server and you have to edit the application, rebuild and redeploy. Fine while the list is short, not a pattern that scales to something like an AI Data Enabler exposing dozens of APIs to LLMs, which is where the next post goes.

 

Solution & references

Build & deploy

This one is a Python FastAPI app with a Node.js approuter in front of it. Prerequisites: Python 3.11+, Node.js 20+ (for the approuter), mbt and the Cloud Foundry CLI with the MultiApps plugin.

Local development:

git clone https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-multiagent-app.git
cd btp-multiagent-app
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
# point the app at your AI Core + MCP servers
export AICORE_SERVICE_KEY='{"clientid":"...","clientsecret":"...","url":"...","serviceurls":{"AI_API_URL":"..."}}'
export MCP_CLOUDFOUNDRY_URL=https://<your-cf-mcp>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/mcp
export MCP_BTP_URL=https://<your-btp-mcp>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/mcp
python -m uvicorn app:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7932

On first chat, each MCP client triggers the OAuth 2.1 browser flow against localhost:3000/callback. Tokens are cached afterwards.

Build and deploy to BTP Cloud Foundry:

mbt build                                        # -> mta_archives/*.mtar
cf login -a <api-endpoint> -o <org> -s <space>
cf deploy mta_archives/<name>.mtar

The MTA contains two modules, the Python FastAPI app (pydantic-agent, started as python -m uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT) and the approuter which binds xsuaa (application plan) plus AI Core (extended plan). MCP server URLs are injected as env vars per module. Reminder: production CF cannot use the localhost OAuth2 callback; switch to client-credentials or pre-provisioned tokens before you promote the deployment.

Code: github.com/lemaiwo/btp-multiagent-app

Previous post: Building a BTP Agent with LangGraph

Next up: making the set of specialists dynamic, so new agents can be created through an admin UI without redeploying the app: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/agentic-ai-on-btp-dynamic-multi-agent-on-demand-with-pydantic-ai/ba-p/14388032#M178664

entry.title: Agentic AI on BTP: Dynamic Multi‑Agent on Demand with Pydantic AI
entry.content:
Introduction

Post one introduced the OData MCP Proxy and gave us five BTP MCP servers by configuration. Post two took those MCP servers and wrapped them in a Pydantic AI multi‑agent application: an orchestrator on top, one specialist per MCP server below. That fixed the tool‑overload problem the original single‑agent design had hit. But it left one uncomfortable property in place: the list of specialists was still hard‑coded. Adding another MCP server meant editing the repo and redeploying. For my own BTP management experiments that is acceptable. For a broader scenario (say, an AI Data Enabler that wants to expose many, many APIs to LLMs safely) it very clearly isn’t.

So in this final iteration I made the agents themselves first‑class, dynamic objects: created, edited, deleted and reloaded without ever restarting the app.

 

Why

Two forces push you towards this design. The first is scale. If every domain, every data product, every API surface deserves its own specialist agent, you don’t want to ship a new release every time someone publishes a new API. You want the platform to accept an agent definition the same way a data platform accepts a new table. The second is governance. Treating agent configurations as data (rather than code) means you can export them, version them, review them, diff them and re‑import them. It also gives you a natural place to attach role‑based access: who can create agents, who can only consume them, who can re‑load the orchestrator.

btp-dynamic-multiagent-app takes those two ideas seriously. Every agent lives as a row in a database. A small admin UI lets an authorised user create, edit and disable agents and a Reload button rebuilds the orchestrator in memory without restarting the container.

 

How

The request path is straightforward. A user hits the approuter, the approuter terminates XSUAA authentication and forwards the request to a FastAPI app. Inside FastAPI there are two logical surfaces: /chat, served by the dynamic orchestrator, and /admin, served by a CRUD API plus a small HTML admin page. Both sit behind the same JWT; the admin routes additionally require an admin scope so the UI can only be used by users with the right role collection.

Figure 1 — btp-dynamic-multiagent-app solution diagram (L2). Editable .drawio version included with this post.

Figure 2 — Screenshot of the admin UI adding a new agent (placeholder — replace before publishing).

The pieces that make it dynamic:

  • agents/db.py: SQLAlchemy models for the agent configuration (name, description, instructions, MCP URL, enabled flag). PostgreSQL in production, SQLite locally so you can iterate without infra.
  • agents/registry.py: a builder that reads the current set of enabled agent records, constructs Pydantic AI agents from them on the fly, and produces a fresh orchestrator with delegation tools pointing at each specialist.
  • agents/admin.py: a REST API for CRUD plus a Reload endpoint that calls into the registry to swap in the newly built orchestrator.
  • agents/auth.py: XSUAA JWT validation and a contextvar‑bound token forwarder so every MCP call made during a request carries the original user’s identity.
  • agents/chat_app.py: a dynamic ASGI wrapper for the chat interface so the underlying agents can be swapped at runtime.
  • templates/admin.html + agents.seed.json: the admin UI and a bootstrap configuration for cold starts.

 

How an operator adds a new agent

In the admin UI an operator clicks "+ New agent", fills in a name, a short description, the system instructions for that specialist and the URL of its MCP server. Saving writes a row. Clicking "Reload agents" asks the registry to rebuild the orchestrator; the new specialist appears in the delegation tool set from the very next chat message. No redeploy. No CF restart (although there is an optional Restart‑app button that can trigger one, if you have a technical‑user service binding to the CF API and you actually want that).

On top of the live CRUD you get export and import of the full agent set as a JSON snapshot. That is small but it is what turns agent definitions from "config in a live DB somewhere" into "versionable artefact you can put in Git". It is also how you seed a new environment from a known‑good baseline rather than clicking your way through twenty entries.

 

Security and identity propagation

Because everything runs on BTP behind the approuter, identity is not something this app invents. Every request already arrives with a validated XSUAA JWT; agents/auth.py pins it into a contextvar for the duration of the request. When a delegated specialist calls its MCP server, the middleware grabs that token and forwards it as a bearer credential. The MCP server authorises using the caller’s actual permissions, not a shared service account. The admin surface is gated by a dedicated "Pydantic Agent Administrator" role collection, so only authorised users can create or modify agents. Normal chat users never see the admin UI.

 

Why this matters for the AI Data Enabler

This is the design I want for any serious "expose‑your‑APIs‑to‑LLMs" story. A centralised MCP server that tries to host tools for every API in the organisation becomes a hot mess very quickly: the tool list explodes, governance becomes all‑or‑nothing, and a single team ends up owning everything. A dynamic multi‑agent app inverts that. Each API surface can publish its own MCP server, owned by its own team, registered as an agent in the platform by its owner and consumed through the same orchestrator. The orchestrator sees a small, stable set of specialists; specialists see small, focused tool lists; identity flows through on every call. You can scale the number of exposed APIs without scaling the complexity of any single component.

 

Lessons learned

  • Treating agent definitions as data is a much bigger win than it sounds like. Export/import, diffing, code review, all of it becomes free once the definition lives in a row instead of in a module.
  • Reloading an agent graph in place is subtle. The trick was to keep the chat ASGI wrapper thin and always resolve the current orchestrator lazily, so swapping the instance is atomic from the request’s point of view.
  • Role‑based admin access from day one avoids retrofits later. If the platform can edit agents, it needs a scope for it, full stop.
  • Using SQLite locally and PostgreSQL on CF kept the inner loop fast without forcing a separate "dev" code path.

 

Solution & references

Build & deploy

Same FastAPI + approuter shape as btp-multiagent-app, with a PostgreSQL service added for the agent registry. Prerequisites: Python 3.11+, Node.js 20+, mbt, and the Cloud Foundry CLI with the MultiApps plugin.

Local development:

git clone https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-dynamic-multiagent-app.git
cd btp-dynamic-multiagent-app
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
# local uses SQLite automatically — no Postgres needed to iterate
export AICORE_SERVICE_KEY='{"clientid":"...","clientsecret":"...","url":"...","serviceurls":{"AI_API_URL":"..."}}'
python -m uvicorn app:app --host 127.0.0.1 --port 7932
# then open http://127.0.0.1:7932/admin to seed agents from agents.seed.json

Build and deploy to BTP Cloud Foundry:

mbt build                                        # -> mta_archives/*.mtar
cf login -a <api-endpoint> -o <org> -s <space>
cf deploy mta_archives/<name>.mtar

The MTA contains the same pydantic-agent Python module and pydantic-agent-approuter Node.js module, and adds a PostgreSQL (postgresql-db, standard plan) resource for the agent registry alongside xsuaa and AI Core. After the first deploy: assign the "Pydantic Agent Administrator" role collection to the users who should be able to create or modify agents, then open /admin to import your agents.seed.json baseline.

Code: github.com/lemaiwo/btp-dynamic-multiagent-app

Editable diagram: 03-btp-dynamic-multiagent-architecture.drawio (shipped alongside this post).

Previous posts in the series: OData MCP Proxy Introduction, btp-agent, btp-multiagent-app.

With this iteration the loop closes: any OData service can become an MCP server with a config file; any MCP server can become a specialist agent with a row in a database and the orchestrator keeps the user experience to a single conversation.

entry.title: From Bengaluru to Koregaon Park to Cline: My D-com 2026 Experience in Pune
entry.content:
April 24, 2026 — a day I'll remember for a while. SAP's d-com event landed in Pune at the stunning Westin Hotel, Koregaon Park, and I had the privilege of being part of it — not just as an attendee, but as a volunteer apprentice alongside the FRE Pune team.

 

Starting the Day as a Volunteer

There's something different about experiencing an event from the inside. As part of my apprentice role, I got to interact with employees before the sessions even began — helping with software installations on desktops that would be used for the hands-on labs. If someone was stuck, I was there. It was a great way to break the ice, meet people early, and genuinely be useful before the day got going.

 

The Hands-On Lab: Vibe Coding with Cline + MCP Servers

The hands-on lab I participated in was a highlight of the day — building an application using Cline with MCP servers, driven entirely by a prompt.

If you haven't tried this yet, it's hard to describe the feeling when you type a prompt and watch an agent do the heavy lifting — discovering files, editing configs, generating code, asking you only when it needs a decision. It's what I'd call vibe coding: you stay in control, the agent does the work.

What I walked away with is a clearer understanding of where I add the most value in this new world. The real skill isn't just writing code — it's knowing what you want, articulating requirements clearly, and validating that what comes out is correct. Once I have that clarity, the speed and intelligence of tools like Claude CLI and Cline become genuinely powerful. I'd already been working this way, but d-com made it feel more tangible and exciting than ever.

 

Most hands-on participants got a bottle with the SAP logo — and I was one of the lucky ones. Always a win.

 

Our Demo: From Manual Onboarding to AI Agents

Vinay and I demo'd our work on SDK adoption at Demo Pod 02 — a session titled "From Manual Onboarding to AI Agents: How We Automated BTP Service Adoption."

Here's the problem we set out to solve: integrating 16 BTP Fabric SDK business services across 100+ applications meant 2–4 hours of repetitive manual work per service, per app — editing mta.yaml, configuring IAS, writing boilerplate, every single time. That's 200+ engineer-hours of work that looks almost identical every time you do it.

Our answer: a multi-agent framework built on LangGraph + MCP, driven through Cline in VS Code.

We built 4 agents:

  • Extensibility Agent — full CAP extensibility onboarding in 10–15 minutes, including IAS config and portal registration
  • Import/Export Agent — annotating CAP entities for data portability with a loop-based annotation menu
  • Event Handler Agent — pick one of 7 proven patterns, get code generated instantly
  • Temporal Agent — previews every file change before writing, rolls back automatically on failure

The key design choice: patterns + LLM, not pure LLM. With 12–16 known services, encoding expert knowledge into YAML skill files means consistency, lower latency, fewer tokens, and no silent mistakes. The framework handles orchestration, pause/resume, checkpointing, and rollback — you just bring the domain knowledge.

The numbers: ~95% time reduction. 3–10 minutes per service, down from 2–4 hours. 200+ engineer-hours saved across 100+ applications. 

9+ more agents are planned. Same architecture, different domain knowledge.

Demo pod speakers got a shirt. I'll take it.

 

A Session That Caught My Eye: Act As for HANA DB

One session that genuinely caught my attention was on "Act As" for HANA DB. It felt familiar — FRE has a similar concept in the works, though for a different use case. Seeing the parallels and the differences in how "Act As" is applied across contexts was a great reminder of how the same pattern can serve very different purposes depending on the domain.

 

The Pune Vibe

There's something special about the Pune office. It's smaller, which means almost everyone knows everyone — and that kind of familiarity creates a really warm energy at an event like this. I got to meet folks from the Supplier Management team I don't usually cross paths with, and those conversations were genuinely enjoyable.

 

Post-Event: The DJ Party

Let's be real — the post-event DJ party was awesome.

Tons of fun, great energy, and a perfect way to wind down after a packed day. These moments of connection outside the sessions are just as valuable as what happens inside them.

 

Shoutout 🙌

A massive thank you to the FRE Pune team for making this happen — it was a pleasure being part of this with you all:

  • Nitin Rakh
  • Vishnu Kumar V H
  • Vinay Sarode

And a special thank you to Harsh Verma and Krishna Bussu for sponsoring the trip — this experience wouldn't have been possible without your support.

 

What I'm Taking Away

D-com 2026 reinforced something I've been learning on the job: the most important thing in AI-assisted development isn't the tool — it's the quality of your thinking. Clear requirements, solid validation, and business understanding are what turn a prompt into something production-ready.

And honestly, d-com didn't just inspire me — it pushed me to act on it immediately. While still in Pune, with a few inputs and suggestions from colleagues, I built a basic Q&A agent using Claude CLI and Cline in just a few hours. That's exactly the spirit of SAP's "All in on AI" motto in action — it's not a solo effort, it's everyone contributing, sharing, and building together.

Once I was back in Bengaluru, I shared it with colleagues for feedback. Those conversations helped me see what could be improved, and I've been iterating on it since. It's a small example of exactly what d-com was preaching: just start, collaborate, get feedback, and make it better. The loop is faster than ever when the tools are this good.

The tools are getting faster and smarter. So the best investment I can make is in getting sharper on the what and the why — and letting Cline, Claude CLI, and the agents handle the how.

Also: Kullad chai from the hotel. A small touch, but a memorable one. 🍵

 

 

 

Thanks to everyone at d-com 2026 Pune for making it a day worth writing about.

#SAPdcom #SAP #AIAgents #VibeCoding #FRE

entry.title: SAP HANA Cloud Intelligent Application - CAP Application with Vector Engine & Generative AI Hub
entry.content:
Prerequisites

  • An active SAP BTP Free Basic Trial account (sign up here)
  • Access to SAP Business Application Studio (included in the trial)
  • SAP AI Core with Extended plan provisioned in the trial environment
  • SAP HANA Cloud instance with the Vector Engine enabled
  • Basic familiarity with JavaScript / Node.js and SAP CAP concepts

What You Will Learn

This lesson is designed to take you from concepts to a running, deployed application. By the end, you will have direct, hands-on experience with each layer of a modern enterprise AI stack on SAP BTP.

After completing this lesson you will be able to:

  • Design a CDS data model that includes SAP HANA Cloud's native Vector(3072) type alongside standard structured data
  • Define OData services in CAP that expose document management and chat pipeline endpoints
  • Implement a RAG engine in Node.js using the SAP AI SDK's AzureOpenAiChatClient to call GPT-4o with context retrieved from HANA
  • Understand how document chunking, vector embedding, and cosine-similarity search work together to produce grounded AI responses
  • Deploy a multi-module MTA application (UI5 Fiori frontend + CAP service + HDI deployer) to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry using an automated deployment script
  • Operate the finished application — upload documents, manage processing states (UPLOADED → PROCESSING → READY / ERROR), start chat sessions, and interrogate document content with natural language

Why these skills matter: RAG is the dominant pattern for safely putting LLMs to work on private enterprise data. By building this yourself — rather than consuming a black-box service — you understand exactly where hallucination risk is managed, how retrieval quality affects answer quality, and how the same HANA database that holds your business data can also power semantic search. That understanding is directly transferable to real customer and internal AI projects on SAP BTP.

The Lesson at a Glance

You clone a pre-scaffolded CAP project from GitHub into SAP Business Application Studio. The project has the full application structure in place — UI5 frontend, service layer, library files, and deployment scripts — but four key files are deliberately left empty. Your job is to implement them. Each file targets one layer of the stack and comes with a clear description of what it does and the exact code to apply.

 

 

 

 

 

Once the four changes are saved you run two shell commands and the complete application — three Cloud Foundry apps, one HDI container, one shared AI Core service binding — is live.

Inside the Four Implementations

Database Schema — db/schema.cds

You define four CDS entities in a single file. The key detail is DocumentChunks, which includes a Vector(3072) column — HANA Cloud's native type for storing the 3 072-dimension embeddings generated by text-embedding-3-large. Storing vectors directly in HANA means your relational joins, access controls, and backup policies all cover your AI data automatically.

Document Service — srv/document-service.cds

This OData service at /api/documents hides the raw chunks association from API consumers — callers interact only with document-level metadata. You declare a cascade-delete action, a status-polling function, and a delete-preview function that tells the UI exactly how many sessions, messages, and chunks will be removed before the user confirms.

Chat Service — srv/chat-service.cds

The sendMessage action is the centre of the lesson. Its return type is deliberately rich — the caller receives not just the AI reply, but a typed array of source chunks with document name and similarity score. This design makes the UI's source-citation panel possible and, more importantly, teaches you how to surface retrieval evidence to end users — a critical pattern for AI transparency in enterprise apps.

RAG Engine — srv/lib/rag-engine.js

This is where everything comes together. The engine uses the SAP AI SDK's AzureOpenAiChatClient (singleton, instantiated once) to call GPT-4o. The prompt is assembled in layers: a system instruction that constrains the model to the retrieved context, the context block itself (each chunk labelled with its source document and cosine-similarity percentage), up to ten prior turns of conversation history, and finally the user's current question.

What the temperature setting teaches you: Setting temperature: 0.3 keeps GPT-4o factual and close to the retrieved context. Higher values produce more creative but less reliable answers — an important trade-off to understand when designing enterprise AI interactions.

From Code to Live App in Two Commands

Once the four files are saved, the automated deployment script handles everything: it validates your configuration, generates the MTA extension, builds all modules, deploys to Cloud Foundry, binds the shared AI Core service, and restages the service app.

Running the Application

The lesson includes a sample product catalog CSV you can use immediately to experience the end-to-end RAG pipeline.

Upload a document — click [+] in the Documents panel and select a PDF, TXT, or CSV file (up to 10 MB):

Watch the pipeline run — status moves from PROCESSING (chunking + embedding in progress) to READY automatically.

Explore with natural language — the lesson uses the included product catalog to demonstrate a realistic business query:

 

What to Look Forward To

This lesson is a foundation, not a ceiling. Once your RAG app is running you will immediately see where the interesting problems lie — and where the next experiments are:

  • Chunk size and overlap tuning — the lesson uses ~1 000-token chunks with ~200-token overlap and sentence-aware boundaries. Changing these parameters has a direct, observable impact on answer quality that you can test immediately with your own documents.
  • Retrieval depth — the default retrieves the top 10 similar chunks per query. Lowering this tightens context; raising it risks adding noise. You can observe the trade-off in the sources panel.
  • Multi-document sessions — the data model already supports reassigning a session to a different document (updateSession action). Experimenting with this reveals how document scope affects grounding.
  • Your own documents — the product catalog is illustrative. The real value becomes visible the moment you upload something from your own domain and start asking it questions that would normally require reading through the whole file.
  • Extending the service layer — the OData services you define in the lesson are deliberate starting points. Adding new actions, custom annotations, or additional entities follows the same CAP patterns you already applied.

 

Start Building Today

The lesson is live now inside the SAP BTP Free Basic Trial. Log in to your trial account, open SAP Business Application Studio, and find the "Build a RAG Application with SAP CAP & HANA Cloud" exercise in the curriculum.

entry.title: ATUL Architecture #1: Why Utilities Need an Intelligent Core on SAP BTP Introduction Utilities are
entry.content:
Introduction

Utilities are entering a decade defined by real‑time operations, distributed energy, and AI‑driven decisioning. Yet most utility architectures were never designed for this level of speed or complexity. The result is a widening gap between what the grid needs and what legacy systems can deliver.

Many challenges come not from SAP itself, but from the surrounding legacy integrations and custom logic that slow down data and processes

As the industry accelerates toward grid modernization, AMI 2.0, and DER integration, A common pattern is becoming clear: architectural constraints around the ERP often limit real‑time operations more than the ERP system itself:

The ERP is no longer the bottleneck—the architecture is.

Even with SAP S/4HANA, utilities may still encounter challenges such as:

  • Delayed response to real-time grid events
  • Data is trapped in silos between the field (OT) and the office (IT).
  • Rigid, high-cost integrations
  • The inability to scale AI beyond pilots

The missing element is an Intelligent Core—a layer that coordinates events, applies business logic, and supports AI‑driven operations.  An Intelligent Core is a real-time orchestration layer that sits between edge systems and ERP, managing events, applying business logic, and enabling AI-driven operations.

1. The Architecture Gap: Complexity Without Coordination

Today’s utility landscape is a tightly coupled mesh of embedded logic, batch processing, and siloed systems. Data moves slowly, intelligence is fragmented, and every change increase complexity.

At its core, this environment is defined by:

  • Embedded Logic: Custom ABAP tightly coupled within ERP
  • Batch Latency: Critical data processed in delayed cycles
  • Siloed Intelligence: AMI, MDM, and grid data isolated from business processes

The result is predictable:

  • Slow response to operational events
  • High cost of change
  • Limited ability to innovate

The challenge is less about individual systems and more about how they coordinate across the landscape.

2. Reimagining the Core: From Record to Orchestration

A modern core is not just a system of record—it is a system of orchestration.

The Intelligent Core acts as the enterprise’s central nervous system, sitting between the edge (meters, grid, customer channels) and the back office (S/4HANA). It performs functions the ERP was never designed to handle:

  • Real-Time Event Orchestration: Reacting to events as they occur
  • Data Normalization: Validating and standardizing inputs before processing
  • Business Rule Execution: Applying logic consistently across systems
  • AI Enablement: Embedding intelligence directly into operational flows

In this model:

  • S/4HANA remains the System of Record
  • SAP BTP becomes the System of Intelligence

3. Why SAP BTP Is the Foundation

The Intelligent Core should never live inside S/4HANA. It requires a platform designed for real-time processing, modular services, and distributed integration.

SAP BTP provides that foundation.

More importantly, it enforces a critical architectural principle:

S/4HANA handles transactions, while BTP handles orchestration, intelligence, and integration.

Figure 1: Intelligent Core Architecture for Utilities

 

This separation enables:

  • Clean Core by Design
  • Event-Driven Agility
  • Modular Services
  • Operational AI
  • Unified Integration Layer

 In this architecture, BTP plays a central role in enabling real‑time orchestration and modular services. In practice, the Intelligent Core leverages SAP Event Mesh for asynchronous event routing, SAP Integration Suite for secure connectivity, and CAP‑based microservices deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. Business rules can be modeled using SAP Business Rules Service, while AI Core orchestrates model execution and inference. Together, these components form a modular, event‑driven stack that scales independently of the ERP.

4.       Conceptual Flow: From Data to Action

The shift in architecture is simple—but powerful:

  • Ingest: Raw events from AMI, grid systems, and customer channels flow into Event Mesh
  • Process: The Intelligent Core standardizes data, applies business rules, and classifies events using AI
  • Execute: S/4HANA receives only clean, validated, business-ready inputs

This changes the game:

  • From raw data → business events
  • From batch → real-time
  • From system-centric → flow-centric

For example, a meter‑read anomaly event published from AMI enters Event Mesh, triggering a CAP service that validates payload structure and enriches it with master data from HANA Cloud. The service then invokes an AI Core endpoint to classify the anomaly type before routing the result to a workflow service that updates the billing process in S/4HANA. Each step is asynchronous, traceable, and governed through BTP’s event and API management layers.

Below diagram shows how AMI Meters and grid systems send event data into SAP BTP’s Intelligent Core for real‑time ingestion, enrichment, AI‑based classification, and workflow orchestration before validated business events reach SAP S/4HANA for transactional processing and analytics.

Figure 2: End‑to‑End Utility Event Flow — AMI → SAP BTP → SAP S/4HANA 

5.  Real-World Impact: Exception Handling Reimagined

The Old Model: Inconsistent AMI data flows into ERP → Billing exceptions increase → Manual teams intervene → Delays and errors compound

The Intelligent Core Model: Events enter the core → Data is normalized and enriched → AI classifies and resolves known patterns → Only true exceptions reach humans

The Result:

  • Higher billing accuracy
  • Reduced operational overhead
  • Faster meter-to-cash cycles

The Intelligent Core integrates seamlessly with SAP Workflow Management and Business Process Automation, allowing utilities to embed exception‑resolution logic directly into operational processes without modifying the ERP.

6.  The Bottom Line

Utilities do not modernize by upgrading ERP alone. They modernize by re-architecting how systems respond, coordinate, and learn.

The shift is clear:

  • From rigid, transaction-centric systems
  • To event-driven, intelligence-driven architecture

SAP BTP becomes a key platform for enabling event‑driven, intelligent operations.

7.  Technical Reference (for practitioners)

  • SAP Event Mesh — Real‑time event routing
  • SAP Integration Suite — Secure connectivity and API management
  • SAP CAP — Modular service development
  • SAP AI Core — Model orchestration and inference
  • SAP Workflow Management — Process automation and exception handling
  • SAP HANA Cloud — Unified data foundation

 

Next in the Series

ATUL Architecture #2 — Event‑Driven Utilities: Designing Real‑Time Flows on SAP BTP.  We’ll walk through:

  • AMI → BTP → S/4HANA event flow
  • Event Mesh patterns
  • Real‑time vs batch
  • A clean, simple event‑flow diagram

entry.title: Why SAP Document AI Matters More Than Most Enterprises Think
entry.content:
Introduction

When everyone gets excited about the same topics, conversations will naturally center around them. Eventually, that excitement will catch on, and you’ll find yourself on the same wavelength as everyone else. Currently, as businesses focus on enterprise AI projects, most discussions revolve around similar themes: copilot, agents, and large language models. However, despite significant investments in AI, many businesses still face a common operational obstacle: documents.

Regardless of the industry, invoices, purchase orders, supplier forms, shipping documents, contracts, and compliance documents flow between departments daily. Each requires some degree of manual verification or validation and often involves multiple systems or workflows. This is where SAP Document AI comes in.

SAP Document AI transforms unstructured business documents into structured operational data, helping businesses modernize their operations. Instead of just digitizing your documents, you can use Document AI to make them useful and functional within your business. Even with advancements in intelligent front-end experiences, the foundational infrastructure supporting these solutions still relies on manual verification, various data extraction methods, and disconnected workflows. Document intelligence within SAP environments has matured, and companies now view it as a key automation initiative rather than a secondary option.

Our Experience

Adopting Document AI can reduce the need for labor to support business functions, leading to more efficient solutions. SAP Document AI addresses a common challenge in enterprise environments: extracting, understanding, and organizing information from business documents. In many companies, important operational data arrives in formats like PDFs, scanned files, emails, invoices, and forms, which ERP systems struggle to process without manual help.

SAP Document AI lowers this friction by using AI to identify document types, extract relevant fields, classify information, and prepare that data for business workflows. This means organizations can automate many document-heavy processes across finance, procurement, supply chain, HR, and other operational areas, rather than having employees manually review documents and transfer information between systems. The value of using Document AI goes beyond faster document processing; it empowers operational workflows to operate with greater speed, consistency, and scalability. As enterprises manage more operational data, minimizing manual document reliance becomes essential for maintaining efficiency.

We had the opportunity to see this first-hand. In this scenario, the company was operating in the oil and gas industry, where customer representatives and field engineers constantly generate operational documents while working across remote locations. Every service ticket, carrier document, export filing, client approval, invoice, or credit memo needed to be attached to the correct Sales Order inside SAP for compliance and operational traceability purposes. The challenge was that these documents arrived through emails in different languages, layouts, and formats, often with little context beyond a subject line. As a result, employees had to manually open every file, identify what type of document it was, locate the Sales Order reference somewhere inside the content, rename the file according to internal naming conventions, and upload it into SAP. At a scale of more than 8,000 documents per month, this translated into over 1,000 hours of manual effort every month, distributed across teams in multiple regions.

The organization already had partial automation in place through legacy capture tools and batch jobs, but the process still depended heavily on human intervention before the automation could even begin. A single typo in a file name or an incorrectly classified document could silently break the workflow and prevent the document from being linked correctly inside SAP. By implementing SAP Document AI on SAP BTP, the process shifted from manual interpretation to intelligent document processing. Incoming documents were automatically picked up through SAP Integration Suite, classified using SAP Document AI, and processed to extract key operational data such as Sales Order numbers, vendor details, amounts, and document dates. The extracted information was then validated and routed directly into SAP workflows, significantly reducing manual effort while improving scalability, consistency, and operational visibility across the entire process.

Why This Matters Beyond Document Processing

What made this project valuable was not just the automation itself. I would say it was rather the impact it had on daily operations. Once documents could be processed and validated in a more consistent way, teams spent far less time trying to interpret files manually or fixing small mistakes that slowed everything down. Workflows became easier to manage across different regions, and the process no longer depended so heavily on people knowing specific naming conventions or internal steps that were never formally documented.

Over time, we also realized that SAP Document AI was doing much more than helping with document processing. It became part of the operational structure supporting the entire workflow. As companies continue investing in SAP Business AI and intelligent workflows, having reliable operational data becomes increasingly important. If documents are still disconnected from the systems around them, scaling automation becomes much harder than expected.

Conclusion

As we said at the beginning, a lot of the conversation around enterprise AI focuses on what people can immediately see: copilots, AI agents, chat interfaces, and new user experiences. At the same time, many operational problems still happen behind the scenes, especially in processes where important business information is buried inside emails, PDFs, scanned files, and manual workflows.

Our experience with SAP Document AI showed us that document intelligence is no longer just a secondary automation tool. It can play an important role in helping businesses create cleaner and more scalable operations. When documents become structured operational data instead of isolated files, workflows move faster, teams spend less time on repetitive tasks, and enterprise systems become easier to scale over time.

entry.title: Data Migration Reimagined – Key Takeaways from SAP Sapphire 2026
entry.content:
🌟 Introduction

Data migration has always been the most critical—and risky—phase of any SAP transformation. But at SAP Sapphire 2026, SAP made it clear: migration is no longer just a technical activity—it is being transformed into an AI-driven, automated, and intelligent process.

With the push toward the Autonomous Enterprise, SAP is redefining how organizations move from legacy systems (ECC or non-SAP) to SAP S/4HANA Cloud—making migration faster, smarter, and less resource-intensive.

🔥 Why Data Migration Is Back in the Spotlight

With the SAP ECC 2027 deadline approaching, organizations are accelerating their move to S/4HANA.
At Sapphire 2026

  • Migration is the gateway to AI adoption
  • Clean, trusted data is essential for Business AI and Joule
  • Cloud transformation (RISE with SAP) depends on efficient data movement

💡 Key message:
👉 No clean data → No effective AI → No intelligent enterprise

🧠 Major Sapphire 2026 Innovations in Data Migration

1. 🤖 AI-Powered Migration Tooling (Game Changer)

SAP introduced agent-led migration tools powered by AI:

  • Automate system analysis
  • Identify and remediate custom code
  • Auto-configure S/4HANA settings
  • Accelerate testing and validation

📊 SAP claims:

  • 35–50% reduction in migration effort 
    • Reference links [linkedin.com], [community.sap.com]

✅ Impact:

  • Faster project timelines
  • Reduced dependency on large migration teams
  • Lower cost of transformation

2. ⚡ Joule-Powered Migration Intelligence

SAP Joule is no longer just a copilot—it’s becoming a migration assistant:

  • Explains legacy code logic automatically
  • Analyzes ATC findings and simplification items
  • Assists in data mapping and transformation
  • Supports decision-making during migration projects

💡 Joule helps teams:

  • Reduce manual analysis effort
  • Improve migration accuracy
  • Speed up complex ECC → S/4 conversions [community.sap.com]

3. ☁️ RISE with SAP as the Migration Backbone

SAP positioned RISE with SAP as the primary path for transformation:

  • Bundles infrastructure, tools, and services
  • Integrates AI-based migration agents
  • Standardizes cloud adoption

👉 Migration is no longer a standalone project—it’s part of a business transformation journey.

4. 🧹 Clean Core Strategy Driving Data Migration

A dominant theme at Sapphire:

  • Remove legacy complexity
  • Standardize business processes
  • Minimize custom code

✅ Benefits:

  • Easier upgrades
  • Faster AI adoption
  • Reduced technical debt

Real-world insights from Sapphire show that:

  • Organizations adopting clean core can deploy AI faster
  • Poor legacy data and customization slow down migration success

5. 📊 Business Data Cloud & Data Readiness

SAP highlighted the growing importance of data platforms:

  • SAP Business Data Cloud unifies enterprise data
  • Provides a foundation for migration + analytics
  • Supports large-scale structured and unstructured data handling

👉 Migration is no longer just ETL—it’s about building a future-ready data ecosystem

🏗️ Evolution of SAP Data Migration Tools

Traditional tools are evolving with intelligence:

Phase Traditional Approach Modern SAP Approach
ExtractManual / batch jobsAutomated discovery with AI
TransformRule-based mappingAI-assisted mapping & cleansing
LoadMigration CockpitSmart orchestration + validation
ValidateManual checksIntelligent anomaly detection

📌 Core tools still include:

  • Migration Cockpit
  • Syniti
  • SAP Data Services
  • SLT / SDI

But now enhanced with automation and AI capabilities

⚠️ Key Challenges Still Remain

Despite innovation, migration isn’t “push-button” yet:

1. Data Quality Issues

  • Duplicate master data
  • Inconsistent formats
  • Missing historical data

💡 Data cleansing often consumes 60% of migration effort in real projects

2. Legacy Complexity

  • Heavy custom ABAP code
  • Poor documentation
  • Multiple disconnected systems

3. Change Management

  • Business process redesign
  • User adoption challenges
  • Skill gaps in cloud + AI

✅ Best Practices for Modern Data Migration

Based on Sapphire insights:

🔹 1. Start with Data Readiness

  • Cleanse and harmonize master data
  • Eliminate duplicates early

🔹 2. Adopt a Phased Migration Strategy

  • Greenfield / Brownfield / Selective approach
  • Avoid “big bang” transformation

🔹 3. Embrace Clean Core

  • Standardize processes
  • Move custom logic to BTP

🔹 4. Use AI Smartly

  • Leverage Joule for analysis
  • Automate repetitive migration tasks

🔹 5. Align Migration with Business Value

  • Prioritize high-impact business processes
  • Link migration to transformation goals

🔮 Future Outlook: Autonomous Data Migration?

SAP’s vision points toward:

  • Self-healing migration processes
  • AI agents managing end-to-end transformation
  • Real-time data validation and correction
  • Continuous migration (not one-time projects)

👉 Migration is evolving from a project into an ongoing capability

🎯 Conclusion

At SAP Sapphire 2026, one message stood out clearly:

👉 Data migration is no longer just about moving data—it’s about enabling intelligence.

With:

  • AI-powered migration tools
  • Joule-driven insights
  • Clean core strategies
  • Cloud-first transformation

SAP is turning migration into a strategic accelerator rather than a bottleneck.

💬 Are you preparing for S/4HANA migration? How are you handling data quality and AI adoption in your projects? Let’s discuss!

 

Thanks,

Venkatesh Golla

entry.title: The Role of Generative AI in SAP Testing: Opportunities and Limitations
entry.content:
Why SAP Testing Has Become More Complex

Modern SAP ecosystems comprise highly interlinked applications where workflows constantly interact with APIs, cloud computing solutions, databases, legacy systems, and other third-party software used in business processes. No longer can organizations limit their testing activities to testing modules or transaction screens. Instead, they need to assess workflow inter-dependencies, integration quality, process integrity, data integrity, and authorization schemes at once.

This complexity has significantly increased demand for enterprise-grade SAP QA testing services. It is necessary for businesses to test vital workflows, including Procure-to-Pay, Order-to-Cash, payroll, account reconciliation, and inventory management, without disturbing business operations. As SAP solutions tend to be updated on an ongoing basis, traditional testing approaches cannot cope with business requirements.

The importance of SAP functional testing has also increased considerably. Functional validation now requires broader coverage across customized workflows, regional business processes, and industry-specific operational requirements. In many enterprise environments, thousands of interconnected transactions and business rules must be validated before deployment.

Organizations also depend heavily on SAP regression testing services to confirm that updates, integrations, or configuration changes do not affect existing functionalities. As SAP ecosystems expand, regression cycles become larger, more repetitive, and increasingly difficult to manage manually. This is where generative AI is beginning to reshape enterprise quality engineering.

Opportunities Created by Generative AI in SAP Testing

Generative AI introduces intelligence into multiple stages of the SAP testing lifecycle by reducing repetitive activities and improving testing precision. Instead of depending entirely on manual scripting and validation, enterprises are using AI-driven models to improve testing scalability and execution efficiency.

According to industry discussions around generative AI adoption in SAP quality engineering, AI-powered systems can generate test cases, analyze business workflows, create synthetic test data, and identify high-risk process areas significantly faster than conventional testing methods. This allows organizations to execute broader validation cycles without proportionally increasing testing effort.

Key Contributions of Generative AI in SAP Testing

AreaTraditional SAP TestingSAP Testing Using Generative AI
Test Case CreationManual scripting and scenario preparation require substantial effort and time.AI generates test scenarios automatically based on workflows, business rules, and historical data.
Test Data ManagementTesting teams rely on static datasets that often require continuous maintenance.AI creates synthetic, production-like datasets for broader validation coverage.
Defect IdentificationDefect detection depends heavily on tester experience and limited test coverage.AI analyzes historical trends and identifies high-risk areas proactively.
Regression TestingLarge regression suites increase execution timelines and resource dependency.AI-driven prioritization improves efficiency across SAP regression testing services.
Automation MaintenanceFrequent SAP UI or workflow changes require script rewrites.AI-enabled self-healing mechanisms adjust scripts automatically.
Integration ValidationManual integration validation becomes difficult across hybrid environments.AI strengthens analysis across SAP integration testing services by identifying dependency risks earlier.
Execution SpeedTesting cycles are slower due to repetitive manual effort.AI accelerates validation cycles and supports continuous testing approaches.

Major Opportunities Enterprises Can Achieve with Generative AI

Reduced Testing Timelines: AI-driven automation significantly reduces the time required for test case generation, execution, and maintenance. This helps enterprises accelerate release cycles while maintaining validation accuracy.

Improved Testing Coverage: Generative AI can simulate multiple business conditions, workflows, and user interactions that are often missed during manual validation processes.

Higher Operational Efficiency: Organizations implementing SAP test automation services can reduce repetitive manual effort and improve productivity across large testing programs.

Better Release Stability: AI-powered analysis helps enterprises identify high-risk workflows earlier. This helps reduce post-deployment defects and operational disruptions.

Scalable Enterprise Validation: Businesses managing global SAP rollouts can execute large-scale testing programs more efficiently without proportionally increasing testing resources.

Limitations and Challenges of Generative AI in SAP Testing

Despite its advantages, generative AI cannot independently replace experienced SAP testing professionals. Enterprise SAP environments contain customized workflows, industry-specific configurations, compliance requirements, and operational dependencies that require a strong business understanding.

Major Limitations Enterprises Must Address

Dependency on Data Quality: AI systems depend heavily on historical datasets and workflow information. Incomplete or inconsistent data can generate inaccurate testing outputs and unreliable recommendations.

Limited Business Context Awareness: AI-generated test cases may overlook nuanced operational scenarios, conditional workflows, or regulatory exceptions. Human validation remains necessary for accurate business process assessment.

Governance and Compliance Risks: Enterprises operating in regulated industries require transparent and traceable testing processes. AI-generated outputs without governance controls may create audit and compliance concerns.

Integration Complexity: Many enterprises operate hybrid environments containing SAP applications, external platforms, APIs, and legacy systems. Integrating AI-driven testing capabilities into existing frameworks often requires infrastructure modernization and process redesign.

Human Oversight Remains Essential: AI can accelerate repetitive testing activities, but it cannot independently evaluate strategic business outcomes, operational continuity, or customer impact. Experienced testing teams remain critical for enterprise validation activities.

Final Say

Generation AI is revolutionizing enterprise quality engineering through better automation scalability, faster test creation, and effective defect analysis in complex SAP environments. With the continuous improvement of enterprise ERP infrastructure and the more widespread application of AI in enterprise functions, organizations are making increasing use of intelligent automation as part of their SAP testing services, which enables more efficient validation and prevents execution bottlenecks.

To fully capitalize on these advances, organizations must balance intelligent automation with robust business expertise and disciplined, governance-driven testing strategies. Success in SAP testing hinges on combining AI-driven speed and efficiency with experienced professionals who safeguard operational continuity, compliance, and business-critical processes. Enterprises should proactively integrate AI capabilities with structured quality engineering practices, ensuring their SAP environments remain resilient, trustworthy, and ready for future demands.

entry.title: Join the SAP SuccessFactors AI User Group
entry.content:
AI is transforming HR faster than ever and staying connected to the conversation matters.

The new SAP SuccessFactors AI User Group is a global, customer-only space designed to help HR professionals stay engaged in AI discussions year-round through peer collaboration, shared insights, and direct access to SAP experts.

Inside the group, customers can:

  • Ask questions and exchange best practices with peers in a GDPR-protected environment (S-user/Universal ID required)
  • Engage directly with SAP HCM AI experts and consultants
  • Discover AI use cases, tips, and ongoing discussions without waiting for the next webinar or event
  • Help build a growing knowledge base the broader customer community can learn from

Whether you're just beginning your AI journey or looking to expand adoption across your organization, this group provides an always-on space to learn, collaborate, and stay informed.

Watch this short 90-second overview demo and join the user group to explore the experience firsthand.

We look forward to seeing you in the conversation.
